content: Human cognition is inherently multimodal: we perceive through multi-sensory channels such as sight, hearing, and touch, and act through different expressive modalities such as speech, writing, and physical manipulation . Coordinating across multiple perceptual and expressive channels imposes greater cognitive load than single-modality tasks . Moreover, when one or more channels are missing or misaligned, that load increases further. For instance, from the perception perspective, a low-vision cook hears sizzling but cannot see browning, which impacts their decision on food states; a doctor conducting a remote consultation has to rely mainly on the patient’s verbal description and limited video, making it harder to observe subtle physical signs such as skin texture or swelling that would normally inform a clinical decision. From the expression perspective, a user who cannot speak needs to issue timing-sensitive commands through typed text; a user who cannot type must perform precise edits via speech alone. In both cases, the mismatch between available channels and task demands amplifies users' cognitive burden. Motivated by these examples, this dissertation makes the following statement: AI-enabled interactive systems can effectively augment human cognition in multimodal workflows by exposing intermediate AI-generated results and surfacing them in controllable interface elements. This allows users to perceive, verify, and adjust outputs with their own sensory and expressive capabilities. The design principle of balancing data-driven system performance and human-centered values such as usability, trust, agency, and safety generalizes across diverse user groups and application domains, from creative video production to accessibility and knowledge work. Specifically, I explore this problem space along four dimensions as shown in Fig. [ref].

Specifically, I explore this problem space along four dimensions as shown in Fig. [ref]. In terms of application areas, the dissertation addresses video editing and consumption (Mimosa, Spica), physical tasks (Aroma), and knowledge work (Transmogrifier). The user groups range from blind or low-vision users (Spica, Aroma) to non-expert users who lack specialized skills (Mimosa) and professional users engaged in knowledge work (Transmogrifier). The data modalities incorporated across the four systems include audio, visual, and textual content. These systems are grounded in fundamental HCI and cognitive science theories including multimodal disambiguation and multimodal interaction theory, mixed-initiative design , and direct-agent manipulation theory.

section: Mimosa: Creating Spatial Audio Effects in Videos
content: Creating spatial audio effects for videos has traditionally required substantial human effort: creators must repeatedly align visual edits with audio quality checks, relying on tight coordination of audio and visual perceptions. To address this problem, Mimosa (Mimosa is an acronym for Magnifying Immersion by Manipulating Objects in Spatial Audio) (Fig. [ref]) explores opportunities to blend AI models into users' multimodal cognitive processes to improve the efficiency while keeping the agency of human editors. Specifically, Mimosa uses an ML pipeline to decompose the task into a sequence of interpretable stages that include object detection, depth estimation, soundtrack separation, and 3D spatial audio rendering. Through a frontend interface, it exposes the intermediate AI-generated results on the graphical user interface (GUI), allowing users to adjust the results through direct manipulation.  We assessed the quality of Mimosa's spatial audio through a subjective evaluation with eight independent external evaluators. Results show that Mimosa-generated spatial audio increased immersion relative to the original audio while preserving realism. We further evaluated usability and usefulness in a user study with 15 participants. Results show that both amateur content creators and users without prior video or audio editing experience are able to use Mimosa easily and effectively.

section: Spica: Enabling Interactive Video Content Exploration for Blind and Low-Vision (BLV) People
content: For blind or low-vision (BLV) individuals, access to video content is essential for information and entertainment needs. The core challenge is converting visual information into accessible formats, most often audio. Traditionally this is done through audio descriptions (ADs), which provide spoken narration of on-screen elements . However, ADs have three limitations: (1) Content depth and relevance: standard ADs emphasize keyframes and often omit objects some viewers care about, creating a mismatch between user intent and system output; (2) Mental load and autonomy: BLV audiences have to understand the original soundtrack and a static AD stream simultaneously and without control over granularity; and (3) Immersive experience: although ADs can be informative, prolonged listening can lead to disengagement, reducing the sense of immersion .  To address these issues, I designed and implemented Spica (Spica is the acronym for System for Providing Interactive Content for Accessibility), an AI-powered system that compensates for missing visual perception while preserving user agency in multimodal exploration. Instead of passively receiving frame-level ADs, users can actively explore objects within frames. They can request detailed, object-specific ADs or sound effects through a touch interface or a screen reader, which reduces cognitive load by letting users control what to attend to and when. Users can also perceive object location through spatial sound to increase engagement. For users with residual light or color perception, Spica overlays a contrasting color mask to enhance visibility. By exposing both frame-level and object-level controls, users are able to control the information depth on their own. Our studies also found that this strategy helped users increase trust in AI-generated descriptions through cross-validation.

section: Aroma: Mixed-Initiative AI Assistance for Non-Visual Cooking by Grounding Multimodal Information Between Reality and Videos
content: In real-world multimodal workflows, users interpret state through multiple sensory cues, such as texture, color, smell, and temperature, to succeed. Cooking in particular demands continuous assessment of food state through these cues . As with Spica, BLV users face significant challenges because visual input is missing, which can compromise safety and reduce independence . In this work, I explored the opportunity to make video recipes accessible. Video recipes are one of the most important sources for providing cooking knowledge , providing rich multimodal information, including visual cues about appearance and auditory cues such as cooking sounds, which help align instructions with real-world observations. Prior work also highlights value beyond sensory content: BLV users report enjoying video recipes but often struggle to access the relevant information . Meanwhile, video tutorials are rapidly expanding, with many cooking lessons, especially from renowned chefs, available only in video form. Despite this growth, video recipes remain largely inaccessible to BLV individuals. To bridge these gaps, I designed and implemented Aroma (Aroma is an acronym for Augmenting Recipe Orchestration with Multimodal Assistance), an interactive AI system that extends compensation into real-world workflows by pairing BLV users' in-situ perceptions with a multimodal agent. Users can access procedural steps, verify cooking state, and receive targeted guidance by querying the video recipe based on their own non-visual perceptions. Following a mixed-initiative design paradigm , the system also monitors cooking state through a wearable camera feed, proactively analyzing the scene and raising alerts when errors or misalignments are detected. This collaboration foregrounds error handling in a high-stakes task, lowers cognitive load through proactive coordination, and adapts to diverse capabilities and preferences in everyday settings.

section: Aroma: Mixed-Initiative AI Assistance for Non-Visual Cooking by Grounding Multimodal Information Between Reality and Videos
content: Following a mixed-initiative design paradigm , the system also monitors cooking state through a wearable camera feed, proactively analyzing the scene and raising alerts when errors or misalignments are detected. This collaboration foregrounds error handling in a high-stakes task, lowers cognitive load through proactive coordination, and adapts to diverse capabilities and preferences in everyday settings. We evaluated Aroma in a user study with eight BLV participants. Each session took place in the participant's own kitchen or another preferred location. Participants used the system to reproduce a dish of their choice from a list of three video recipes. From these results, we assess usability, identify key contextual challenges BLV users face when cooking with video-based instructions, and propose design implications for interactive AI systems that improve video accessibility and support activities of daily life.

section: Transmogrifier: Brushing and Interpretive Linking for Knowledge Work
content: The final project extends from video applications into a more general domain: knowledge work, where knowledge workers often repurpose content across formats (e. g. , notes to slides, reports to figures) . This project presents Transmogrifier, a prototype that supports dynamic transformation and continuous editing of multimodal content. In Transmogrifier, we explored whether AI can serve as a partner that helps externalize, translate, and stress-test those schemas by linking evidence across representations, proposing alternative formulations, and preserving provenance as ideas evolve. We term this new interactive paradigm "interpretive linking. " On the technical side, Transmogrifier performs transformation across text, charts, and images. The probe lets users create linked content blocks on an infinite canvas, each annotated with a meta-description (e. g. , "bullet points," "bar chart," "cover illustration"). These meta-descriptions act as soft constraints that guide transformations. The system includes version history to support minimal yet meaningful propagation of edits, and visual traceability mechanisms (e. g. , highlighting, bolding) to reveal how changes propagate across representations. Cross-modal pipelines combine large language models with segmentation and diffusion-based inpainting to localize visual edits while maintaining stylistic coherence. To understand the implications and applications of interpretive linking for content creation and consumption, we conducted a study with six content creation professionals from diverse backgrounds. Participants generated two dozen application concepts, ranging from responsive smart documents, where edits in one section automatically propagate to related sections, to asymmetric group ideation tools that allow collaborators to engage with the same idea through different modalities.

section: Transmogrifier: Brushing and Interpretive Linking for Knowledge Work
content: To understand the implications and applications of interpretive linking for content creation and consumption, we conducted a study with six content creation professionals from diverse backgrounds. Participants generated two dozen application concepts, ranging from responsive smart documents, where edits in one section automatically propagate to related sections, to asymmetric group ideation tools that allow collaborators to engage with the same idea through different modalities. We employed a probe-to-prototype methodology, rapidly prototyping participant-proposed concepts using our technology probe. From these prototyped concepts, we gathered participant feedback that illuminated key design considerations for building applications that leverage interpretive linking.

This dissertation makes the following system contributions and insights to design human-centered AI systems that support multimodal workflows. - Mimosa, a human-in-the-loop pipeline that spatializes monaural video audio and exposes intermediate representations for direct editing of spatial layouts. It identifies example applications for blending AI models into multimodal workflows. - Spica, an interactive video-access system for BLV users that supports both frame-level and object-level exploration through layered audio descriptions and spatial cues. It explores opportunities for dynamically compensating for missing perceptions of users. - Aroma, a mixed-initiative cooking assistant that aligns video recipes with BLV users' multimodal perceptions (sound and touch) via on-demand guidance and proactive state monitoring. It exemplifies the feasibility of building human-AI collaborative systems in the physical world, extending beyond audio and visual modalities. - Transmogrifier, a technology probe that performs interpretive linking to propagate semantically grounded edits across text, charts, and images.

The remainder of this dissertation is organized as follows. - Chapter [ref] introduces background and related work, mainly focusing on literature across HCI, AI, and cognitive science on multimodal interaction, human-centered AI, creativity support, and accessibility. - Chapters [ref] present four systems that augment users' cognitive processes across different application domains: spatial audio creation for videos (Mimosa, Chapter [ref]), interactive video exploration for BLV users (Spica, Chapter [ref]), mixed-initiative cooking assistance (Aroma, Chapter [ref]), and content repurposing across modalities and formats (Transmogrifier, Chapter [ref]) - Chapters [ref] discuss limitations of the four systems, reflect on rethinking the classic "man-machine symbiosis" in the generative AI era based on the design implications throughout the presented systems, and propose future research directions along both data-driven and human-centered axes.

This chapter reviews three areas of research that ground this dissertation. The structure is shown in Fig. [ref]. Specifically, Section [ref] examines key problems of human-AI interaction such as error-handling problem, trust and agency, and specifically in video creation domain. Going beyond, Section [ref] reviews literature investigating situations where one or more perceptive modalities of users are missing, and how assistive technologies compensate for absent perception in media consumption and physical activities of daily life. Section [ref] broadens the scope to the gap and coordination between multimodal content and multimodal cognitive capabilities in Gen-AI powered knowledge work, where the central challenge is maintaining semantic coherence as ideas are repurposed across diverse representations and modalities.

section: From Cognitive Theory to Applications in Human-AI Collaborative Systems for Multimodal Workflows
content: Human cognition is fundamentally multimodal, and classical cognitive and HCI theories provide the theoretical bedrock for understanding how people process information across sensory channels. Early models of human information processing established the foundational view that perception, cognition, and action form tightly coupled stages, each with measurable capacity limits. People continuously integrate visual, auditory, haptic, and proprioceptive signals to perceive and act in the world . Dual coding theory offers a cognitive explanation for this advantage: verbal and nonverbal channels are processed in partially independent systems, and redundancy across channels strengthens encoding, recall, and transfer . Cognitive load theory also adds a resource constraint: working memory is limited, and extraneous processing demands can overwhelm it . Meanwhile, Mayer's multimedia learning principles show that coordinated visual and verbal materials reduce extraneous load relative to equivalent single-channel presentations . Hutchins's distributed cognition extends the frame further, arguing that cognitive work is distributed across individuals, artifacts, and the environment, so that an interactive system becomes an active participant in the cognitive process rather than a passive display . These cognitive foundations strongly motivates the formation of some classical theories in HCI community, which together serve as the foundation of this dissertation. Horvitz's principles of mixed-initiative interaction formalize the balance between user-driven and system-driven action: the system may proactively intervene, but the user retains authority to override, query, or redirect. Amershi et al. codified a broader set of 18 guidelines for human-AI interaction, including making system capabilities clear, supporting efficient correction, and scoping services appropriately when confidence is low .

section: From Cognitive Theory to Applications in Human-AI Collaborative Systems for Multimodal Workflows
content: Amershi et al. codified a broader set of 18 guidelines for human-AI interaction, including making system capabilities clear, supporting efficient correction, and scoping services appropriately when confidence is low . Together, these frameworks surface three cross-cutting concerns that recur throughout this dissertation: error-handling problem, as AI models inevitably make mistakes, users need transparent mechanisms to detect and correct them ; cognitive load management, as multimodal workflows impose coordination costs that systems should actively mitigate; and adaptation to diverse user capabilities, since users vary in which modalities they can access, their expertise, and their preferences. Meanwhile, Shneiderman's foundational concept of direct manipulation argued that interfaces enabling users to directly act on visible objects with immediate, reversible feedback reduce cognitive overhead and increase engagement, a principle that Norman later popularized through the concepts of affordances and mappings in everyday design . Building on these theoretical achievements, in the area of multimodal interaction in HCI, researchers demonstrates how combining input channels yields richer, more natural, and more robust human--computer interaction. Bolt's seminal "Put-That-There" system showed that combining voice and gesture produces richer, more natural interaction than either modality alone , establishing a design vision that has shaped decades of subsequent work . Natural user interfaces further extended this tradition to touch and gesture-based interaction, emphasizing intuitive, body-centric input that lowers the threshold for novice users . Oviatt's work on mutual disambiguation further demonstrated that multimodal input can reduce recognition errors by allowing modalities to complement and constrain each other .

section: From Cognitive Theory to Applications in Human-AI Collaborative Systems for Multimodal Workflows
content: Natural user interfaces further extended this tradition to touch and gesture-based interaction, emphasizing intuitive, body-centric input that lowers the threshold for novice users . Oviatt's work on mutual disambiguation further demonstrated that multimodal input can reduce recognition errors by allowing modalities to complement and constrain each other . Together, these results establish that multimodal systems can effectively reduce the cognitive burden of interaction and improve system robustness. These insights from multimodal interaction have inspired a great number of system-centric research, especially in creativity support tools: interactive systems that augment human creative capability . The HCI community has a long tradition of building such tools, from early interactive sketching environments for rapid UI prototyping to modern systems that map how computational assistance can inspire and expand creative practice across domains . A recurring finding is that the most effective tools do not simply automate subtasks; they restructure the creative process so that users can focus on higher-level intent while the system manages lower-level execution. The rapid scaling of Transformer-based models has accelerated this trend dramatically: large language models demonstrated emergent few-shot and chain-of-thought capabilities , reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligned these capabilities with human intent , and multimodal models unified visual and linguistic representations . With the advent of AI-enabled co-creation, an expanding family of tools now pairs human creators with AI models across sketching , music composition , writing , and video production . Three design strategies have emerged as central to this partnership. First, users should maintain control and consistently play a "leading role": Oh et al. found that satisfaction increases when the system provides detail on demand rather than acting autonomously .

section: From Cognitive Theory to Applications in Human-AI Collaborative Systems for Multimodal Workflows
content: First, users should maintain control and consistently play a "leading role": Oh et al. found that satisfaction increases when the system provides detail on demand rather than acting autonomously . Second, systems should foster accurate mental models of the AI's behavior by employing pipelines whose stages mirror human reasoning processes, making the system's logic legible . Third, systems should provide efficient mechanisms for handling errors in AI-generated outputs---a concern that becomes especially pressing in iterative creative workflows where a single uncorrected mistake can propagate through downstream edits . Among those work, video creation is one of the most representative domain as it naturally incorporates audio and visual modalities: a single video interleaves a visual track, an audio track, and a temporal structure; editing it requires a creator to reason simultaneously about what is seen, what is heard, and how the two align over time. Sound design alone illustrates the challenge: matching a sound effect to an on-screen event demands audiovisual synchronization, spatial reasoning about source position in the frame, and aesthetic judgment about how the resulting mix feels to a viewer . Traditionally, generating good spatial audio effects requires modeling the head-related transfer function (HRTF) and associating each sound source in a monaural recording with its visual counterpart using positional information extracted from visual frames. In AI communities, models have been invented to make this process automated . However, despite the stength of spatial audio, widespread adoption remains limited by expertise barriers and opaque end-to-end pipelines that offer users little control over intermediate decisions, the "black-box" workflow that the creativity support literature warns against.

section: From Cognitive Theory to Applications in Human-AI Collaborative Systems for Multimodal Workflows
content: In AI communities, models have been invented to make this process automated . However, despite the stength of spatial audio, widespread adoption remains limited by expertise barriers and opaque end-to-end pipelines that offer users little control over intermediate decisions, the "black-box" workflow that the creativity support literature warns against. My work Mimosa addresses this gap by using spatial audio creation as a lens into the broader challenge of blending AI into human multimodal creative cognition process. By decomposing the editing pipeline into interpretable, editable stages, such as object detection, depth estimation, soundtrack separation, and 3D spatial tagging, it instantiates the co-creation design strategies above and provides a concrete setting in which to study how users maintain agency, build mental models, and handle errors when AI mediates a tightly coupled audiovisual creative workflow.

section: Multimodal Perception in Accessibility: Compensating for Missing Sensory Modalities
content: The previous section reviews work on how AI augments human cognition when all channels are available. A more fundamental challenge arises when one or more modalities are absent. When the cognitive system loses a source of corroborating evidence and must reconstruct meaning from the channels that remain . This is the daily reality for people with sensory impairments: blind or low-vision (BLV) individuals having difficulty consuming the visual content that dominates modern media, while Deaf and Hard of Hearing (DHH) users miss auditory cues that convey emotion, urgency, and spatial context. More broadly, any situated task in which sensory input is restricted, whether by impairment, environmental constraint, or device limitation, creates a gap between the information a task requires and the information the user can perceive . Prior research on assistive technology for people with sensory impairments falls into two broad categories: (i) facilitating cognitive processes to compensate for limited sensory input , and (ii) assisting users in overcoming physical barriers that hinder task execution , including augmentative and alternative communication for non-verbal users , eye-tracking for hands-free manipulation , and accessible robotics . Key theoretical frameworks inform the first category, which is the focus of this dissertation. Mixed-initiative design is especially relevant because individuals with sensory impairments may be unable to independently perceive errors that require system-initiated intervention . Multimodal disambiguation highlights the importance of integrating information across whatever modalities remain available. Building on these principles, researchers have combined auditory and haptic cues for BLV navigation , explored gesture-based control on assistive devices , augmented audiovisual information in VR for low-vision users , and added haptic and captioning support for DHH media consumption .

section: Compensating for missing vision in media consumption.
content: A prominent instance of the compensation problem is video accessibility. In an era where video is a dominant medium for news, education, and entertainment, ensuring that BLV individuals can access this content is a matter of social equity . Audio descriptions (ADs)---narrated accounts of visual content inserted during pauses in dialogue---remain the primary means of access . Existing guidelines advocate for precise, concise, and objective narrations that illuminate crucial visuals without interrupting dialogue , but the limited availability and high cost of professional AD services constrain widespread implementation . Manual authoring tools such as LiveDescribe and Rescribe , peer feedback systems , and accessibility checks have made AD creation more approachable, yet they remain time-consuming and do not scale . Automated AD generation using deep learning shows promise but often lacks the contextual richness needed for BLV audiences . Beyond passive consumption, interactive exploration has emerged as a powerful complement to linear ADs. ImageExplorer introduced hierarchical strategies for BLV users to deepen their understanding of static images; Slidecho aligned extracted slide elements to presenter speech for BLV learners; Fan et al. improved the accessibility of screen-shared presentations by enabling concurrent exploration of slide content ; AVscript adapted temporal navigation for non-visual video editing. Tools for exploring real-world objects also inform this space: Seeing AI ( provides camera-based environmental information, CueSee guides low-vision product exploration via a head-mounted device, Slide Rule enables audio-based multi-touch interaction, and RegionSpeak supports touch-based image exploration through crowd-annotated regions.

section: Compensating for missing vision in media consumption.
content: improved the accessibility of screen-shared presentations by enabling concurrent exploration of slide content ; AVscript adapted temporal navigation for non-visual video editing. Tools for exploring real-world objects also inform this space: Seeing AI ( provides camera-based environmental information, CueSee guides low-vision product exploration via a head-mounted device, Slide Rule enables audio-based multi-touch interaction, and RegionSpeak supports touch-based image exploration through crowd-annotated regions. Unlike static content, however, video introduces unique challenges: narration follows a temporal order, so a tool must support comprehension of scene transitions while consistently conveying non-visual elements; and video consumption must optimize for both understanding and engagement ---a dual objective that prior research has shown is difficult to achieve simultaneously. Mental fatigue from sustained AD use, confusion from low-quality descriptions, and reduced spatial awareness can diminish engagement even when understanding improves . Factors such as scene detail , delivery style , and emotional tone further shape the BLV viewing experience. Technical advances are also expanding the design space for accessible video consumption. Sequential approaches include automated AD generation and layered audio descriptions that support non-linear exploration . Scene segmentation techniques that maintain visual consistency across shots provide a structural foundation for these methods. Non-sequential approaches include hierarchical video summaries and Video Question Answering (VideoQA), where models generate natural language answers grounded in video content . Recent foundation models trained on large audiovisual corpora continue to push the boundaries of open-ended video understanding .

section: Extending compensation into real-world physical tasks.
content: The compensation challenge intensifies when it moves from media consumption to situated physical activity, where the environment is dynamic, multisensory, and less forgiving of error. Cooking is an exemplary case: it is a naturally multimodal activity that requires integrating visual, tactile, auditory, and olfactory cues to assess food state, manage timing, and coordinate parallel operations . For BLV individuals, cooking is also a critical daily activity that supports independence and quality of life . Two clusters of challenges have been identified. The first concerns recipe access and interpretation. Text-based recipes are accessible through OCR and text-to-speech but often omit critical visual context---what "cook until golden brown" looks like remains unclear . Video recipes are rich in multimodal cues but lack structured navigation, under verbalized visual content, and impose high cognitive load ; despite these barriers, BLV users find them entertaining and inspiring . BLV cooks prefer structured, chunked formats, hands-free interaction, and sensory checkpoints (e. g. , smells, sounds) that bridge the gap between written instruction and physical reality . The second cluster concerns real-time state recognition. A contextual inquiry by Li et al. identified eight classes of contextual information that BLV people actively seek during cooking, including spatial layout, object status, and dynamic properties such as temperature or doneness. BLV users develop embodied workarounds, placing a spoon at a known angle to anchor spatial memory, but these strategies are fragile in multitasking or shared kitchen environments. Systems such as CookAR augment appliance affordances through head-mounted AR for low-vision users, while OSCAR provides context-aware step-completion feedback by tracking object status changes.

section: Extending compensation into real-world physical tasks.
content: BLV users develop embodied workarounds, placing a spoon at a known angle to anchor spatial memory, but these strategies are fragile in multitasking or shared kitchen environments. Systems such as CookAR augment appliance affordances through head-mounted AR for low-vision users, while OSCAR provides context-aware step-completion feedback by tracking object status changes. In this dissertation, Spica and Aroma use, respectively, accessible video consumption and assisted cooking as lenses into the broader problem of compensating for missing modalities. Spica generates hierarchical, object-level audio descriptions and spatial sound cues that give BLV users interactive control over the depth and timing of visual information, studying the tension between understanding and engagement when vision is absent. Aroma extends this compensation into a situated, real-world workflow: it pairs BLV users' non-visual perceptions with real-time visual analysis through a mixed-initiative architecture, studying how human sensory strengths and AI visual capabilities can be combined to bridge the gap between recipe instruction and physical cooking state.

section: Linking and Coordinating Multimodal Representations in Knowledge Work
content: The first two sections considered tasks in which humans integrate or compensate for multiple sensory channels in real time. A different but equally fundamental multimodal challenge arises in knowledge work, where content is represented in diverse representations, e. g. reports, slides, charts, images, code, that serve different communicative purposes and address different audiences. On the other hand, in knowledge a group of content representations often share a similar topic. Previous work has shown that distributing information across complementary channels reduces cognitive load , and different collaborators may need different formats to match their roles, preferences, or cognitive styles . The key problem; however, is coordination: when one representation changes, every related artifact needs to be located, re-examined, and updated in a form appropriate to its own modality and intent. This coordination burden is a recurring source of error and inefficiency in knowledge work. The visualization community has long addressed a structured version of this problem through brushing and linking . Brushing selects or highlights a subset of data in one view; linking ensures that corresponding data are simultaneously revealed or filtered in other coordinated views . This mechanism greatly enhances exploratory analysis by reducing manual cross-referencing and supporting richer sensemaking . Its success, however, rests on a critical assumption: different views share stable identifiers, e. g. rows, keys, or relational schema, that make correspondence explicit and linking deterministic . Knowledge-work artifacts rarely meet this assumption. A single sentence in an abstract may correspond to multiple paragraphs in the body; a chart might summarize only a portion of a discussion; an illustration may convey tone rather than literal content.

section: Linking and Coordinating Multimodal Representations in Knowledge Work
content: Knowledge-work artifacts rarely meet this assumption. A single sentence in an abstract may correspond to multiple paragraphs in the body; a chart might summarize only a portion of a discussion; an illustration may convey tone rather than literal content. The question shifts from "which marks correspond to the same data item" to "which parts are semantically related across representations and granularity levels. " This gap is amplified by the pervasiveness of content repurposing in professional practice. Knowledge workers routinely rework long-form text into concise summaries , shift between sketches, comments, and executable code , or transform data into charts . Prior HCI research has explored several paradigms for supporting these practices: "fluid documents" for adaptive co-editing , cross-device interaction techniques for moving content across form factors , persistent and reusable interactions in computational notebooks , collaborative platforms that maintain shared grounding across users and representations , and empirical studies that characterize repurposing as shaped by social dynamics and organizational context . These approaches, however, typically rely on explicit structure, predefined transformations, or developer-specified mappings, limiting their flexibility when artifacts differ in modality and communicative intent. Recent advances in large pretrained generative models have fundamentally expanded what transformations are feasible. Large language models enable text-to-text and text-to-code generation , and emerging agentic paradigms allow models to synergize reasoning with action , autonomously learn to invoke external tools , and simulate social behavior in open-ended environments ; diffusion models enable high-quality text-to-image synthesis ; and emerging research explores text-to-audio and text-to-video generation .

section: Linking and Coordinating Multimodal Representations in Knowledge Work
content: Recent advances in large pretrained generative models have fundamentally expanded what transformations are feasible. Large language models enable text-to-text and text-to-code generation , and emerging agentic paradigms allow models to synergize reasoning with action , autonomously learn to invoke external tools , and simulate social behavior in open-ended environments ; diffusion models enable high-quality text-to-image synthesis ; and emerging research explores text-to-audio and text-to-video generation . These capabilities suggest a fundamental shift: instead of encoding rigid mappings, systems can attempt to infer semantic relationships and produce context-appropriate updates. Yet generative transformation introduces new risks, e. g. semantic drift, inconsistency, and hallucination, because mappings are probabilistic and generation is non-deterministic. The core challenge therefore becomes not only producing transformations but maintaining semantic coherence as multiple artifacts evolve over time. In this dissertation, Transmogrifier uses knowledge-work repurposing as a lens into this coordination problem. It introduces interpretive linking---a generalization of brushing and linking in which content changes, rather than selections, are propagated across representations by identifying semantically related elements and interpreting edits in one artifact to update others while preserving each artifact's communicative role. This reframing treats cross-representation coordination as the management of coherence under ambiguity, connecting the visualization tradition of coordinated views with the generative AI capabilities that now make cross-modal, cross-format coherence tractable in everyday knowledge work.

section: Mimosa: Blending AI into Multimodal Creative Cognition in Spatial Audio Creation for Videos
content: (This chapter is modified from the conference paper: Zheng Ning, Zheng Zhang, Jerrick Ban, Kaiwen Jiang, Ruohong Gan, Yapeng Tian, and Toby Jia-Jun Li. MIMOSA: Human-AI Co-Creation of Computational Spatial Audio Effects on Videos. In Proceedings of the 16th Conference on Creativity & Cognition (C&C '24), Chicago, IL, USA, 2024.

Video creation is among the most cognitively demanding multimodal tasks, requiring creators to reason simultaneously about what is seen, what is heard, and how the two align over time. This chapter presents Mimosa (Mimosa is an acronym for Magnifying Immersion by Manipulating Objects in Spatial Audio), the first system in this dissertation, which addresses the challenge of blending AI into human multimodal cognition without displacing human intent---Theme 1 of this dissertation. Spatial audio allows listeners to perceive the position of sound sources in three-dimensional space through surround sound , and has been shown to significantly improve immersion, engagement, comprehension, and retention of video content . However, adoption of spatial audio among video creators, especially amateurs, remains limited. Recording spatial audio requires specialized hardware such as in-ear microphones or 360^ microphones . Millions of existing videos contain only monaural or stereo audio . Even when spatial audio is available, post-processing demands specialized tools and expertise . End-to-end machine learning models have made progress in generating spatial audio from monaural video , but they fall short of the practical needs of content creators for two reasons. First, audio effect creation is a personalized, subjectively evaluated process , while ML models optimize for "ground-truth" quantitative metrics; their black-box nature prevents users from validating or revising outputs. Second, creators often seek to tailor effects beyond realistic reconstruction---adjusting volumes, redistributing sound in 3D, or experimenting with settings ---yet ML models are limited to approximating how audio would sound if recorded with spatial equipment. These limitations directly instantiate the three recurring challenges of this dissertation. Error handling: the black-box pipeline provides no mechanism for users to discover or repair mistakes in generated audio.

These limitations directly instantiate the three recurring challenges of this dissertation. Error handling: the black-box pipeline provides no mechanism for users to discover or repair mistakes in generated audio. Cognitive load: editing spatial audio for video requires tight audio--visual coordination, with discrepancies between modalities diverting attention and increasing mental effort . Diverse user capabilities: amateur creators vary widely in expertise with audio editing, necessitating tools that support both novice-friendly exploration and expert-level control. Mimosa addresses these challenges by decomposing the spatial audio generation task into interpretable, editable stages---object detection, depth estimation, soundtrack separation, audio tagging, and 3D spatial rendering---and exposing the intermediate results through a direct-manipulation interface. Rather than relying on an opaque end-to-end model, Mimosa employs a carefully designed audiovisual pipeline (Fig. [ref]) that aligns its stages with the user's cognitive workflow. The intermediate outputs, such as the type and position of each sounding object and the separated soundtracks, are organized and presented through interactive panels that allow users to manipulate spatial locations and audio attributes of individual sound sources. This design keeps intermediate representations visible (reducing cognitive load), makes model outputs inspectable and editable (supporting error handling), and enables novice-friendly exploration alongside expert control (accommodating diverse user capabilities).

The intermediate outputs, such as the type and position of each sounding object and the separated soundtracks, are organized and presented through interactive panels that allow users to manipulate spatial locations and audio attributes of individual sound sources. This design keeps intermediate representations visible (reducing cognitive load), makes model outputs inspectable and editable (supporting error handling), and enables novice-friendly exploration alongside expert control (accommodating diverse user capabilities). The contributions of this system are: (i) a human-centered AI system, Mimosa, that supports spatial audio creation through an interactive, interpretable pipeline; (ii) empirical evidence from subjective and usability evaluations showing how Mimosa enhances creative flexibility and perceived immersion; and (iii) design insights into enabling user-driven spatial audio workflows by aligning AI-generated results with human cognitive and creative processes.

To illustrate how Mimosa supports the interplay between AI-generated spatial audio and human creative control, we present a scenario in which an amateur video creator, Lucy (she/her) (A pseudonym for demonstrating the usage scenario. ), wanted to enhance the soundtrack of a video featuring two music players performing flute and violin in a room with immersive spatial audio effects. An example screenshot of a video frame is similar to Fig. [ref]. Lucy started by launching Adobe Premiere Pro (Pr) and loading the target video. She selected the desired clip for editing and launched Mimosa from the Extension menu in Premiere Pro. The system automatically started processing the video; when it finished, the main interface of Mimosa (Fig. [ref]) appeared, allowing her to proceed with editing the default generated spatial effects. Next, she played the video with the default generated spatial audio effects to detect potential issues. As the video played, the positions of dots (Fig. [ref]-E), spheres (Fig. [ref]-A), and numeric values (Fig. [ref]-C) changed automatically over time, illustrating the detected sounding objects' positions. She could control play/pause, playback progress, and move backward/forward one second using the control buttons (Fig. [ref]-D), similar to a typical video player. The real-time volume indicators (Fig. [ref]-A) visualized the volume from different channels to improve her understanding of the spatial effect at specific times. She could also check whether the dots representing the inferred spatial positions of sounding objects aligned with the actual visual objects in the frame. During playback, Lucy noticed that the spatial sound of the flute was incorrect and wanted to repair it. There were three types of errors in general. First, if the spatial positions of the flute and violin were reversed, she could re-specify their correspondence by editing the sounding object name in Fig. [ref]-A.

First, if the spatial positions of the flute and violin were reversed, she could re-specify their correspondence by editing the sounding object name in Fig. [ref]-A. Second, if the volume of either instrument was too loud or too soft, she could adjust the volume of each instrument separately using the volume controller (Fig. [ref]-D). Third, if the correspondence was correct but the position prediction was inaccurate, she could: (i) drag the dot representing the flute toward the desired direction in the video display panel; (ii) manipulate the sphere corresponding to each sound source in the 3D panel, which also allowed her to adjust the distance from the viewer to the sounding object; or (iii) manually change the numeric 3D position in the audio properties display and control panel. As a video creator, Lucy also wanted to augment the spatial effect beyond the estimated visual positions by following her creative ideas. Specifically, she hoped to increase the distance between the two sound sources so that the audience could more distinctly perceive sounds from separate directions. To achieve this, she simply moved the red dot further to the left and the yellow dot further to the right. She also wanted to test how different positions of the two players would affect spatial effects and how the audience would perceive the scene from various angles. She could create desired spatial effects by manipulating the position of each sounding object using any of the three approaches described above. Additionally, she could change the viewing point in the 3D panel by adjusting the camera object's position and angle. The spatial audio effects were re-rendered in real time. Once satisfied with her spatial audio creation, she pressed the SAVE button on the interface. The edited spatial audio tracks were loaded into Premiere Pro automatically, allowing her to continue editing other aspects of the video.

Once satisfied with her spatial audio creation, she pressed the SAVE button on the interface. The edited spatial audio tracks were loaded into Premiere Pro automatically, allowing her to continue editing other aspects of the video. This scenario illustrates Mimosa's three-phase workflow of detect, repair, and augment, which mirrors how creators naturally approach audio editing while supporting the error handling, cognitive load reduction, and creative flexibility goals of this dissertation.
The design of Mimosa is informed by three technical challenges and corresponding design goals that directly connect to the dissertation's recurring themes.

Drawing on previous studies on creativity support tools and computational approaches in audiovisual contexts , we identified the following technical challenges for designing Mimosa: - TC1: Limitations in fully-automated ML models. Current state-of-the-art ML models have limited performance in generating realistic and immersive spatial audio from videos containing only monaural or stereo audio. The quality of generated spatial audio is significantly affected by training data, video quality, and video scenarios. This challenge corresponds to the dissertation's concern with error handling: when models are imperfect, users need mechanisms to discover and correct mistakes. - TC2: Lack of user control in the generation process. Due to the black-box nature of current end-to-end models, users cannot easily repair errors in the generated effect or control the generation process to customize the final output. This relates to diverse user capabilities: users with different expertise levels need different levels of control. - TC3: Increased cognitive burden for multimodal data. Users must handle both audio and visual data while editing audio effects, and discrepancies between modalities divert attention . This issue becomes more pronounced when visual and audio information are inconsistent in their spatial positions, directly mapping to the dissertation's concern with cognitive load. In light of these challenges, we identified three design goals: - DG1: Allow users to effectively repair errors in model-generated spatial audio effects. The system should provide user-interpretable intermediate results of ML models, allowing users to verify results and rectify errors. - DG2: Provide flexibility for augmenting and creatively modifying spatial audio effects.

The system should provide user-interpretable intermediate results of ML models, allowing users to verify results and rectify errors. - DG2: Provide flexibility for augmenting and creatively modifying spatial audio effects. The system should allow users to go beyond the visual relative positions of sounding objects, offering ample expressiveness such as flexibly moving individual sound sources and adjusting reference points. - DG3: Coordinate user perceptions and cognition while handling audiovisual data. The system should provide effective and easy-to-learn interaction strategies to reduce cognitive load when users edit audio effects in the video context.

The architecture of Mimosa consists of two layers. When a video is loaded, the audiovisual spatializing pipeline (illustrated in Fig. [ref]) runs in the backend to process the video and generate a default spatial audio effect. The pipeline also produces intermediate data, enabling users to identify and address any issues in the generation process. [! htb]    On the second layer, users can modify the intermediate results through the system's main interface (Fig. [ref]), which consists of three main components: (i) The video playback and 2D sounding source overlay panel (Fig. [ref]-A), which allows users to manipulate the inferred position of each individual sound source in a 2D space that references the video frame by moving corresponding overlay colored dots. (ii) The simulated 3D manipulation panel (Fig. [ref]-B), designed to facilitate user interaction with sound source positioning within a simulated 3D space; it also allows for modifications to the reference point by manipulating the camera object through movements or rotations. (iii) The audio properties display and control panel (Fig. [ref]-C), which allows users to verify and manually specify pairings between soundtracks and their associated visual objects, provides a comprehensive overview of audio properties, and enables users to interact with these properties.

The 2D and 3D direct manipulation panels visually represent sound sources as dots in 2D and spheres in 3D within distinct reference systems. These panels enable users to adjust the spatial positions of sound sources to fix errors in the ML model's predictions or create customized spatial effects. The 2D panel allows users to manipulate sound sources within the original video frame using dot-like visual representations, as illustrated in Fig. [ref]-E. Each colored dot represents a specific type of sounding object, with its default [x, y] position predicted by the ML model and its size determined by the predicted depth ([z]) from the imaginary camera position. Users can reposition the objects by dragging the corresponding dots to adjust the [x, y] coordinates as needed. In contrast, the 3D manipulation panel (Fig. [ref]) operates within a simulated 3D space, independent of the original video frame. Predicted 3D positions of sounding objects are represented as colored spheres. The panel gives users a greater degree of creative freedom: users can manipulate objects within the open 3D space, modify the point of recording (Fig. [ref]-B) through rotation or translation (Fig. [ref]-C), and rotate the entire simulated space to view object positions and the camera from various angles. The goal of the 2D and 3D panels is to allow users to select preferred reference systems for balancing the trade-off between error correction and creative support. The 2D panel allows users to easily correct errors in predicted spatial positions by aligning dots with actual sounding objects in the video frame, but limits depth correction and experimentation from different perspectives. The 3D panel replaces the original video frame reference with a flexible 3D coordinate system and viewing point, allowing users to manipulate objects within an open space and incorporating diverse interactive strategies to support creativity.

The video panel displays the video and includes several interactive elements. Users can select the audio channel mode from a drop-down list (Fig. [ref]-B). Mimosa supports four modes: monaural (1-channel), stereo (2-channel), quadraphonic (4-channel), and 5. 1-channel audio. The number of channels corresponds to the number of volume indicators in Fig. [ref]-A. Each bar represents the sound intensity of its respective channel. For example, in a 5. 1-channel setup, the bars at the upper right, center, upper left, bottom left, and bottom right indicate the sound intensity for the front left, center, front right, rear left, and rear right channels, respectively. These channel volume indicators aid users in the error discovery process by visually representing sound intensity across each channel. Additionally, the toggle in Fig. [ref]-C allows users to enable or disable the use of model-predicted spatial coordinates. When enabled, the system automatically interpolates the position of a sounding object between two frames where the user modifies positions, reducing the effort required for manual editing of individual frames. When disabled, the spatial coordinates remain unchanged until the user moves the object, providing full user control.

section: Audio Properties Display and Control Panel
content: This panel allows users to correct misalignment between separated soundtracks and corresponding visual objects. By default, the identified sounding object name appears in Fig. [ref]-A. Users can select the correct visual object name from a drop-down menu if the default is incorrect. Users can also click the button (Fig. [ref]-B) to change the color of each sounding object's visual representation in the 2D and 3D manipulation panels. The panel displays the spatial location of individual sounding objects in a numeric format (Fig. [ref]-C). Users can modify the position of each object by directly editing the corresponding numeric value. Users can also refer to the waveform of each separated sound source (Fig. [ref]-E) to facilitate navigation to specific timestamps, and adjust the volume of each sound source via the button (Fig. [ref]-D).
This section describes the backend audiovisual spatializing pipeline and the system's implementation details.
The audiovisual pipeline (Fig. [ref]) generates spatial audio effects for each sounding object through three stages.

section: Inferring the 3D positions of visual objects in 2D videos
content: The video is first sampled into individual frames. A pre-trained object detector extracts the 2D positions and respective categories of objects within each frame (Fig. [ref]-B). A pre-trained image depth estimation model generates a pixel-level depth map for each frame (Fig. [ref]-A). Using the object's 2D position, we query the depth map to obtain depth information for each object, allowing us to accurately determine and track the 3D position of each object over time. The object detector was built using the Faster R-CNN architecture and implemented with the Detectron2 framework (. The model was pre-trained on the MS-COCO dataset and does not require fine-tuning when integrated into our system.

section: Separating individual soundtracks and aligning them with visual counterparts
content: At the audio level, a sound separation model extracts individual soundtracks from the original sound mixture. Each soundtrack contains sound from a single sounding object (Fig. [ref]-C). To align auditory information with visual information, an audio tagging model (Fig. [ref]-D) predicts the sound types for each source (e. g. , speech, telephone, music). The separated soundtracks are then mapped to the corresponding category of visual objects, using their names as bridges.

This module takes the separated soundtrack for each sound source, mixes them together based on the corresponding 3D positions, and distributes the mixed signal into different channels depending on the speaker setup. The module is built using the PannerNode object in the WebAudio API (, which simulates sound effects in a simulated 3D space given the 3D coordinates of the sounding object and the listening position. Since the original PannerNode only supports two-channel audio rendering, we combined two PannerNodes to compute spatial effects separately for sources from the front and the back.

The front-end of Mimosa is hosted on a virtual machine through Google Cloud. The Adobe Premiere Pro plugin for Mimosa is built with the Adobe Common Extensibility Platform (CEP) (. Mimosa's front-end UI was implemented in React ( rather than using the native CEP APIs in Premiere Pro to enable flexible interactions. The backend spatializing pipeline runs on a workstation with an AMD Ryzen Threadripper 3960X CPU and an NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPU.

We evaluated Mimosa through two complementary studies: a technical evaluation assessing the quality of the generated spatial audio, and a user study evaluating usability, usefulness, and user experience.

This independent evaluation sought to answer two questions: (i) How effective is the audiovisual spatializing pipeline compared to an offline end-to-end model? (ii) How is the quality of the spatial audio created by users using Mimosa?

We recorded six sample videos across various scenarios, each with original spatial audio effects serving as a contrast condition. Each video is approximately one minute long. Detailed information is shown in Table [ref]. Each video clip with ground-truth spatial audio was recorded with an iPhone 13 camera and a ZOOM H3-VR microphone (, a popular economic 360^ audio recorder with a four-capsule ambisonic microphone array.

We recruited eight independent evaluators to subjectively evaluate different types of audio. Evaluators were 20 to 30 years old and were recruited from the local community. They had no previous experience with Mimosa and did not participate in the user study, ensuring unbiased ratings. Each evaluator reported having experience consuming videos with and without spatial audio effects. Each video had five different audio types: - (1): Raw audio (RA): the pre-recorded original spatial audio. - (2): Monaural audio (MA): the pre-recorded original audio played in a single-channel setting (no spatial effect). - (3): Spatial audio generated by an offline end-to-end model (OA). - (4): Default-generated spatial audio by Mimosa's pipeline without any user intervention (DA). - (5): Spatial audio created by users using the Mimosa system (UA). We shuffled the order of video clips and presented them to evaluators in random order. Each evaluator rated about 12 videos, each with five different audio types. Evaluators rated each video on Immersion and Realism separately on a 7-point semantic differential scale. Immersion evaluates the extent to which the spatial audio effect was perceptible and how well spatial locations could be inferred from the audio. Realism focuses on identifying any distortion or dissonance that could be perceived as unreal.
