We found that videos with spatial audio after user editing with Mimosa (UA) were more immersive than all other audio types. A Friedman test showed a significant difference among the mean immersion scores (p<0. 001). Pairwise comparisons with Wilcoxon signed-rank tests confirmed that differences between UA and all other audio types were statistically significant (p<0. 001). The immersion score of DA (=4. 47; =0. 94) was close to RA (=4. 51; =1. 19), with no significant difference (p=0. 54). This suggests that spatial audio generated by Mimosa's pipeline in a fully automated way achieves immersion comparable to raw audio recorded with a 360-degree audio recorder. User validation and revision further improved immersion.

This suggests that spatial audio generated by Mimosa's pipeline in a fully automated way achieves immersion comparable to raw audio recorded with a 360-degree audio recorder. User validation and revision further improved immersion. Notably, the default-generated effects were better in both realism and immersion than OA generated by an end-to-end ML model. We also found that spatial effects after user editing with Mimosa (UA) compromised realism compared to raw audio (RA) but showed improvement over the fully automated outcome (DA). A Friedman test identified a significant difference in Realism (p<0. 001). RA received the highest mean score (=6. 03; =0. 59), followed by MA (=5. 68; =0. 33). This suggests that computationally generated spatial audio inevitably introduces some distortion, leading to decreased user-perceived realism.

Informed by prior research in measuring creativity support and heuristic evaluation of digital tools , we conducted an in-person lab study (The study protocol has been reviewed and approved by the IRB at our institution. ) to evaluate the usability, usefulness, and user experience of Mimosa. The study addressed the following research questions: - RQ1:: How usable is Mimosa in assisting users to edit spatial effects and support their expressiveness? - RQ2:: What is the user perception towards the interactive strategies of Mimosa? - RQ3:: How would users utilize Mimosa to augment the spatial audio effects while editing?

15 participants (9 men, 6 women) aged 20--30 were recruited from the local community. 8 participants were amateur video creators who had posted videos on websites such as YouTube, while 7 did not consider themselves video content creators. 6 participants were experienced with video editing tools such as Adobe Premiere Pro; 7 were novice users, and 2 had no video editing experience. Each participant was compensated with \15 USD for their time.

[t]   The study was conducted in a usability lab (Fig. [ref]), equipped with a set of Logitech Z606 5. 1 surround speakers ( that provided audio playback during the study. At the beginning of each session, the participant signed the consent form and completed a demographic questionnaire. After a brief introduction, the participant watched a five-minute tutorial video on how to use Mimosa and a one-minute 5. 1 channel spatial audio test video to check the surround speakers. Each participant completed two tasks for each of the six video clips. The order of clips was randomized, but the tasks followed the same order. The first task evaluated Mimosa's capability for facilitating human-AI collaboration: the user was asked to create realistic spatial sound effects by repairing inaccuracies and errors in the default generated spatial audio (DA). The second task evaluated Mimosa's capability for flexible customization: after a warm-up with two guided scenarios ("Please move the Saxophone player so that it gradually moves away from you" and "Please turn your face back to the basketball player"), the user was asked to create a customized spatial audio effect to their own liking. The study session ended with a post-study questionnaire and a 10-minute semi-structured interview. Participants rated statements on usability and user experience on a seven-point Likert scale (Fig. [ref]). Following established open coding methods , an author conducted a thematic analysis of interview transcripts to identify insights related to user experiences, challenges, system usability, and suggestions for new features. All 15 participants successfully completed both tasks for all six video clips.

[! htb]     Overall, participants were satisfied with their experience with Mimosa. They found it easy to use, useful, expressive, and capable of generating immersive spatial audio effects. Specifically, Mimosa scored 6. 47 (=0. 52) in "Mimosa is useful", 6. 20 (=0. 68) in "the spatial audio effects created through Mimosa is immersive", 6. 27 (=0. 96) in "Mimosa allows me to freely create the spatial audio effects that I like", and 5. 87 (=1. 06) in "Mimosa is easy to use". Regarding interaction features, the 3D direct manipulation panel scored 6. 07 (=1. 09), and the 2D dots visualization overlay scored 6. 53 (=0. 83$). spatial audio effects with ease. Participants stated that they could "quickly get familiar with the functions of the system" (P1) and "speed up the editing process after finishing editing the first several videos" (P13). Most participants mentioned that the spatial effects after editing were more immersive, citing examples such as "I can clearly feel the car is moving from left to right" (P8) and "When I moved the Saxophone to my back, the sound was actually coming from that position" (P3). However, several participants noted issues such as back-to-front sound transitions not being as natural as expected, reporting a "crunch sound" (P9) during playback. Some users also mentioned that the visual position of sounding objects in the interface did not always align with the auditory position they perceived. creativity and expressiveness by intuitive manipulation and real-time playback. Participants expressed that they could easily "test a variety of different audio settings" (P14) due to the flexible manipulation strategies. P11 mentioned that "Aligning the dots and the sounding objects in the video frame cost nearly no labor to me, so I felt really excited playing around with different settings.

Participants expressed that they could easily "test a variety of different audio settings" (P14) due to the flexible manipulation strategies. P11 mentioned that "Aligning the dots and the sounding objects in the video frame cost nearly no labor to me, so I felt really excited playing around with different settings. " Participants appreciated the flexibility of augmenting spatial effects by intentionally "increasing the distance between the two sounding objects or bringing them closer to the reference point" (P3). The 3D manipulation panel was particularly valued for enabling flexible changes to the viewing point. P15 stated that "It allows me to imagine I can walk into the video scene and pretend to be at a particular position to hear the music instruments playing. " Several participants emphasized that hearing effects after making changes further encouraged them to test more scenarios. P8 expressed: "Getting instant feedback is super important in the editing process... this allows me to do the editing and evaluating tasks simultaneously. " audio error by using visual cues. We found that participants, especially amateur content creators, relied on visual cues to discover and repair errors in the generated spatial audio. Most users first discovered inaccuracies by noticing the difference between the position of colored dots and the corresponding visual objects. P13 noted that, compared to listening to spatial audio and trying to identify errors, looking at the misalignment of representative dots allowed him to "easily find out unnoticed errors by just `hearing'. " P15 added: "(the strategy) makes fixing the error easy and intuitive, as I just need to drag the dots to make it overlap with the objects. " This finding aligns with the mutual disambiguation paradigm from multimodal interaction theory : by representing audio results visually, Mimosa augments users' audio perception with visual information, making error discovery more tractable.

" P15 added: "(the strategy) makes fixing the error easy and intuitive, as I just need to drag the dots to make it overlap with the objects. " This finding aligns with the mutual disambiguation paradigm from multimodal interaction theory : by representing audio results visually, Mimosa augments users' audio perception with visual information, making error discovery more tractable. choose the manipulation method depending on personalized user needs. Mimosa provides three distinct approaches for manipulating sounding object positions (Fig. [ref]-E, Fig. [ref], and Fig. [ref]-C). Most participants found dragging in the 2D or 3D panels more user-friendly than editing numerical values. P2 noted: "I only used the numerical values as a reference, because the spatial effect changes were more immediate when directly moving the dot or spheres. " However, some participants preferred numerical input, especially for precise creative positioning. P1 stated: "I felt more confident editing spatial audio effects when using the numerical input, especially when I tried to create a new spatial audio effect and wanted to position different objects at various places. " Some participants (P6, P10, P11) reported no strong preference and mixed all three methods, demonstrating how the system accommodates diverse user capabilities and preferences. for new features. Participants offered recommendations for potential new features. P8 proposed adding a drop-down menu to select specific objects when their visual representations overlap in the 2D or 3D panels. P7 suggested incorporating an action history panel with timestamps to enable users to review and modify previous actions.

section: Error Discovery and Repair as a Foundation for Human-AI Co-Creation
content: Effective human-AI collaboration depends on mutual understanding of goals and trust between user and system . Yet machine learning models inevitably introduce prediction errors that threaten both, making the design of collaborative systems uniquely challenging. Our study results with Mimosa demonstrate that robust error discovery and repair mechanisms are essential in co-creation contexts where humans and AI build iteratively on each other's intermediate outputs. Mimosa addresses this challenge by decomposing the task into a multi-step pipeline that produces interpretable intermediate results at each stage, rather than relying on an opaque end-to-end model. This design gives users the opportunity to review, validate, and correct model outputs at each step. For instance, the 2D panel overlays inferred sounding object positions onto the original video, enabling users to visually verify spatial alignment and repair misplacements through direct manipulation. As discussed in Section [ref], participants found these interaction strategies made error discovery and repair straightforward — a finding further supported by the improvement in realism scores from DV to EV in our evaluation. This design draws on classic theories of multimodal interaction, applying the principle of mutual disambiguation to the context of human-AI co-creation. Because the model's output is auditory, validating its alignment with visual information in the original video is cognitively demanding. Mimosa alleviates this burden by simultaneously representing generated spatial audio visually on the 2D and 3D panels, allowing users to compare across modalities at a glance. For editing, users can either manipulate visual indicators directly or adjust auditory properties through the soundtrack information panel.

section: Error Discovery and Repair as a Foundation for Human-AI Co-Creation
content: Mimosa alleviates this burden by simultaneously representing generated spatial audio visually on the 2D and 3D panels, allowing users to compare across modalities at a glance. For editing, users can either manipulate visual indicators directly or adjust auditory properties through the soundtrack information panel. This dual-modality design lets users choose whichever interaction channel is most natural for their current goal, while the synchronized cross-modal representation makes it easier to spot discrepancies in either modality.

section: Supporting Creative Exploration Beyond Model Predictions
content: Creative tools promise users the expressiveness to explore beyond what is strictly realistic. This poses a tension when incorporating machine learning into co-creation workflows: models are trained to predict "ground truth" learned from data, which can constrain the creative space. While recent generative models and commercial systems such as DALL-E can produce impressive results autonomously, they often lack the fine-grained user control necessary for true co-creation. Mimosa illustrates an approach that uses model predictions as a starting point for user-initiated augmentation and creative exploration. Throughout the pipeline, model outputs are presented in ways that align with users' existing mental models of the task. For example, the 3D panel visualizes inferred sounding objects and the camera reference point within a simulated 3D space. Users can reposition sounding objects, move the camera, or change the viewing angle — operations that closely mirror real-world spatial manipulation and therefore carry a low learning curve. Users can also directly adjust the intensity of individual sound sources through the soundtrack information panel. This design enables users to offload laborious lower-level sub-tasks to AI while retaining control over higher-level creative decisions — a key principle for AI systems that aim to augment rather than replace human cognition.

Mimosa is a human-AI co-creation tool that enables amateur users to computationally generate and interactively manipulate spatial audio effects in videos with only monaural or stereo audio. It demonstrates how AI can be blended into a tightly coupled audiovisual creative workflow while preserving user agency. By decomposing an opaque end-to-end pipeline into interpretable, editable stages and exposing intermediate results through direct manipulation, Mimosa addresses all three recurring challenges of this dissertation: it supports error handling by making model decisions visible and correctable at each stage, reduces cognitive load by representing audio information visually so that cross-modal comparison replaces demanding auditory inspection, and accommodates diverse user capabilities by offering multiple interaction strategies---2D dragging, 3D manipulation, and numeric input---at varying levels of precision. The technical evaluation confirmed that Mimosa's pipeline produces spatial audio comparable in immersion to professional recordings, while the user study demonstrated that the system is usable, expressive, and capable of supporting creative spatial audio workflows.

section: Spica: Interactive Video Exploration for Blind or Low-Vision Users
content: (This chapter is modified from the conference paper: Zheng Ning, Brianna L. Wimer, Kaiwen Jiang, Keyi Chen, Jerrick Ban, Yapeng Tian, Yuhang Zhao, and Toby Jia-Jun Li. SPICA: Interactive Video Content Exploration through Augmented Audio Descriptions for Blind or Low-Vision Viewers. In Proceedings of the 2024 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI '24), Honolulu, HI, USA, 2024.

Video content is an essential resource for information, education, and social participation, yet the visual modality that carries most of a video's meaning remains inaccessible. This chapter presents Spica (Spica is the acronym for System for Providing Interactive Content for Accessibility), an AI-enabled tool to support interactive video exploration for blind or low-vision (BLV) users. The standard accessibility mechanism for video is audio description (AD), spoken narration that conveys visual elements to BLV audiences . For professionally produced media, creating ADs requires significant collaborative effort among content creators, description writers, narrators, and audio engineers. For user-generated content on platforms such as YouTube and TikTok, adoption rates remain far lower . End-to-end machine learning models have begun to automate AD generation , but their applicability remains limited due to accuracy concerns. Even well-crafted ADs have three intrinsic limitations rooted in their static nature: - Content depth and relevance. AD norms emphasize clarity and significance, leading descriptions to cover only keyframes and pivotal information . Yet the depth and quantity of content desired for any given frame can vary widely among users . A gap thus emerges between what a BLV user wants and what ADs provide, reducing engagement and limiting fair access to visual information. - Mental load and autonomy. Prior research indicates that BLV audiences experience higher mental load than sighted viewers when consuming video . Sighted viewers freely choose which visual cues to attend to, whereas BLV users must parse the original soundtrack and the AD stream concurrently through the audio channel alone, with no control over what details to focus on. - Immersive experience. Existing ADs are designed primarily to facilitate understanding .

- Immersive experience. Existing ADs are designed primarily to facilitate understanding . While they succeed at conveying factual content, prolonged listening can lead to boredom and disengagement, undermining the viewing experience. These limitations map directly onto the three recurring challenges of this dissertation. Error handling: when AI models generate descriptions, inaccuracies can mislead users who have no independent visual channel to cross-check; the system must provide mechanisms for users to discover and assess potential errors. Cognitive load: BLV users already face higher mental effort from single-channel information consumption; adding more description without giving users control over timing and granularity only compounds the problem. Diverse user capabilities: BLV users span a wide spectrum of vision conditions, technology proficiency, and information preferences, so a one-size-fits-all AD is inherently insufficient. Spica addresses these challenges by augmenting, rather than replacing, existing ADs with interactive, layered exploration. Instead of passively consuming predetermined narration, users can actively explore individual objects within video frames at their own pace. They can access detailed object-specific descriptions or sound effects through either a touch interface or a screen reader, perceive object locations through spatial audio, and, for users retaining some light or color perception, benefit from high-contrast color overlays. Critically, Spica preserves existing ADs as a baseline and layers additional AI-generated content on top, giving users the agency to decide when and what to explore further.

They can access detailed object-specific descriptions or sound effects through either a touch interface or a screen reader, perceive object locations through spatial audio, and, for users retaining some light or color perception, benefit from high-contrast color overlays. Critically, Spica preserves existing ADs as a baseline and layers additional AI-generated content on top, giving users the agency to decide when and what to explore further. The contributions of this chapter are: (i) Spica, an AI-enhanced interactive system that enables BLV users to explore video content through layered audio descriptions and spatial sound effects; (ii) empirical evidence from a user study with 14 BLV participants demonstrating improved understanding and immersion; and (iii) findings and design implications for future interactive accessible video systems that foreground user agency and cognitive accessibility. [!

To illustrate how Spica supports interactive video exploration, consider Lucy, a user with acquired blindness who retains minimal light and color perception. Lucy wants to watch a baking tutorial video. She starts by pressing the SPACE key. As the video reaches a segment with a native audio description, Spica automatically pauses and vocalizes the AD: "A young man is standing behind the table holding a pot. " If Lucy chooses not to use any interactive feature, the video plays exactly as it would with conventional ADs. However, the AD piques her curiosity: she wants to know what the pot looks like and whether other objects are on the table. She places her finger on the video frame and scans across it. When she touches the pot, Spica reads: "This is a pot, located at the top right, description: a silver metal pot with melted butter inside. " Simultaneously, a colored overlay highlights the pot's shape, leveraging her residual color perception, and a spatialized boiling sound plays from the corresponding direction. Next, Lucy wonders whether she missed any important cooking steps. She presses the up-arrow key, and Spica jumps to the previous keyframe, reading its scene description. She can continue pressing arrow keys to navigate forward or backward through keyframes, each time hearing the timestamp and scene description. For hands-free exploration, Lucy presses RETURN to switch to keyboard-based object navigation. Using arrow keys, she cycles through all objects in the current frame, hearing each description and spatial sound effect in turn. When satisfied, she presses SPACE to resume playback. This scenario illustrates two complementary exploration dimensions that define Spica's interaction model: temporal navigation across visual scenes and spatial exploration of objects within each frame.

When satisfied, she presses SPACE to resume playback. This scenario illustrates two complementary exploration dimensions that define Spica's interaction model: temporal navigation across visual scenes and spatial exploration of objects within each frame. Together, they give BLV users the agency to customize their video consumption experience, directly addressing the dissertation's concerns with cognitive load (users control what to attend to), error handling (users can cross-check descriptions across frames), and diverse capabilities (multiple input modalities accommodate different preferences).
The design of Spica is organized around the three limitations of static ADs identified in the introduction, each connecting to a recurring dissertation challenge.

Spica consists of two components (Fig. [ref]): [! htb]    Pipeline. The backend pipeline (Fig. [ref]) produces the layered content that enables interactive exploration: (1) Scene analysis identifies keyframes and generates or retrieves corresponding scene descriptions; (2) Object segmentation detects and segments objects of interest within each frame; (3) Object description generation produces a raw caption for each object using an image captioning model applied to the frame with the object's background blurred, then refines the caption through GPT-4 to ensure consistency with the scene description; (4) Object sound effect retrieval queries the Freesound database ( using keywords from the generated description; and (5) Object depth estimation predicts the depth of each object relative to the frame, which, combined with its 2D position, enables spatialized sound effects and position descriptions. Interactive Application. The interface (Fig. [ref]) has three panels: (1) a video player (Fig. [ref]-A) that supports playback and allows users to explore objects through touch or keyboard, rendering a high-contrast color overlay on the selected object; (2) a frame-level caption list (Fig. [ref]-B) presenting keyframe timestamps and scene descriptions for temporal navigation; and (3) an object-level caption list (Fig. [ref]-C) displaying labels and descriptions of individual objects within the selected frame.

Traditional video players offer only fixed-interval skipping (e. g. , forward or backward by ten seconds). For BLV users, this approach is disorienting because they cannot see what scene they have landed in. Informed by prior work showing that BLV users find scene-based navigation more intuitive than timeline-based navigation , Spica allows users to jump directly between keyframes using arrow keys. Each transition automatically adjusts the video to the relevant timestamp and reads the scene description aloud, providing continuous orientation within the video narrative. The keyframe detection mechanism segments the video according to four rules: (1) frames containing native audio descriptions; (2) frames where the visual scene differs significantly from the previous frame; (3) frames where object count or types have changed substantially; and (4) a maximum 5-second interval cap to ensure exploration density. Within each segment, the pipeline selects a representative keyframe based on an object score (object diversity relative to the segment, measured using a set-distance metric ) and a semantic score (uniqueness of the scene description computed via Sentence-BERT ). Frames with native ADs are treated as keyframes by default.

section: Spatial Exploration of Objects within Frames
content: Prior studies have shown that enabling BLV users to interact with visual content hierarchically can improve comprehension while reducing information overload . Building on this finding, Spica provides two complementary object exploration techniques: - Touch-based exploration. Users scan the video frame with their finger. Touching an object triggers its description, a high-contrast color overlay, and a spatialized sound effect. This technique conveys both the 2D position and intuitive size of each object. - Keyboard-based exploration. Users cycle through objects sequentially using arrow keys. This technique ensures comprehensive coverage: no object is missed, and users can quickly return to a specific object. Users can alternate between techniques freely. Each object description begins with a unique index (1 to n) to minimize redundancy when multiple similar objects appear. Informed by recent research on spatial audio for enhancing BLV viewing experiences , Spica retrieves an object-specific sound effect and spatializes it according to the object's estimated 3D position, enhancing spatial awareness and engagement. For low-vision users who retain some light or color perception, a high-contrast color mask highlights the selected object in the frame.

The pipeline uses an off-the-shelf image captioning model to generate frame-level descriptions for frames lacking native ADs. For object-level descriptions, the same model is applied to a version of the frame where the object's background is blurred, embedding scene context into the caption. These raw captions are then refined through GPT-4, which improves consistency between object descriptions and the frame description and removes artifacts (e. g. , "a close up of," "blurry image of"). Table [ref] illustrates three examples of this refinement process. The frontend of Spica was built with React and the backend with Flask. Google text-to-speech API was used to vocalize generated descriptions. The object detector used Mask R-CNN pre-trained on the MS COCO dataset (80 common object categories). A confidence threshold of 0. 9 was applied to filter low-confidence predictions, and objects whose height or width fell below 0.

We evaluated Spica through two complementary studies: a technical evaluation assessing the quality of the ML pipeline's outputs, and a user study with 14 BLV participants evaluating usability, usefulness, and user experience.

To validate the pipeline, we conducted an offline benchmark evaluation on 20 videos from the QueryYD dataset. ( These videos were sourced from YouTube with audio descriptions contributed by sighted volunteers. They cover diverse topics including movie clips, vlogs, TV show clips, and advertisements. For comparison, we used a baseline pipeline that directly connects a state-of-the-art object detection model to a state-of-the-art image captioning model without the refinement and context-integration steps of Spica's pipeline.

Two annotators manually labeled the ground truth for each keyframe using LabelMe. The overall precision of Spica's pipeline was 0. 939 (SD=0. 03), and recall was 0. 791 (SD=0. 07). Incorrect and missing predictions were primarily due to occlusion or object categories absent from the training set. Each video exhibited similar precision and recall, demonstrating robustness across scenarios. The result is shown in Table [ref].

Two graders rated each object-level description on a seven-point scale considering correctness, naturalness, and comprehensiveness. Inter-grader agreement was strong (Cohen's weighted =0. 71). The baseline achieved an average rating of 3. 91 (SD=1. 24), while Spica's pipeline achieved 5. 10 (SD=1. 29). A one-way ANOVA confirmed a significant difference (p<0. 001). As illustrated in Table [ref], Spica's pipeline produces more natural, context-aware descriptions by removing irrelevant phrases, aligning object descriptions with scene context, and integrating positional information. We conducted a within-subjects lab study (The study protocol was reviewed and approved by the IRB at our institution. ) with 14 BLV participants to evaluate Spica against a baseline condition (original ADs only). The study addressed three research questions: - RQ1:: What is the user perception of the usability of Spica? - RQ2:: How do individual interactive features influence users' information access and immersion? - RQ3:: How do BLV users use Spica to explore video content, especially to determine when to pause and what to explore?

14 BLV participants (P1--P14) were recruited from social media and university mailing lists. The average age was 32. 1 (SD=12. 2). Participants had various vision conditions ranging from legal blindness to blindness with some light/color perception to total blindness, including both congenital and acquired conditions. Each participant was compensated \60 USD. The demographics information of participants are shown below.
