The study was conducted remotely on Zoom. Participants used tablets with keyboards or laptops with touchscreens to alternate between touch-based and keyboard-based exploration. Each participant consumed two different videos in each condition (baseline and Spica), with the order counterbalanced. Six videos (1. 5--3 minutes each) from the QueryYD dataset were used, each with cohesive storylines and high-quality original ADs. Each session lasted approximately 60 minutes: consent and demographics, a 15-minute tutorial, the video consumption tasks (with understanding and immersion rated on a 7-point Likert scale after each video), a post-study questionnaire on usability and usefulness, and a 15-minute semi-structured interview. Two researchers analyzed interview transcripts through affinity diagramming and independently coded participant behaviors from video recordings using established open-coding methods . The coding process was conducted based on the following hypotheses: - [H1:] Users' active behaviors are correlated to specific playback positions in a video. - [H2:] User-initiated explorations happen when users perceive missing or conflicting information in a video.

[! htb]     usability and usefulness. Participants found Spica effective and easy to use, rating it 6. 29 (SD=0. 99) for ease of use, 6. 79 (SD=0. 43) for usefulness, 6. 57 (SD=0. 65) for providing additional information, and 6. 29 (SD=0. 83) for improved immersion (Fig. [ref]). Comparing conditions, Spica received significantly higher ratings than the baseline for both understanding (=6. 11 vs. \ =4. 79; Wilcoxon signed-rank p=0. 033) and immersion (=6. 25 vs. \ =4. 29; p=0. 046$). Participants valued the system for "giving more options for blind people" (P3) and providing "depth information to help better understand the video" (P9). navigation supports narrative control. Participants found that temporal frame exploration helped them develop deeper connections with the storyline. P1 valued the ability "to go back and investigate the scene that I missed during the initial viewing," and P4 expressed that it gave her "a lot more control over my own viewing experience. " Participants also used the feature to skip unengaging scenes (P4, P5) and felt "confident" (P9, P11) about not missing important content. Concerns focused on narrative coherence disruption from frequent pauses and the time investment required for thorough exploration. exploration complements frame-level understanding. Overall, 39. 4\ sound effects enhance immersion without increasing cognitive load. Most participants found the object-specific sound effects useful (mean effectiveness: 5. 86/7, SD=1. 51; spatial effects: 5. 21/7, SD=1. 67). Importantly, participants reported that sound effects did not add mental burden; rather, they "add extra fun" (P1) and helped develop richer scene understanding. Twelve of 14 participants found the retrieved effects more helpful than the original soundtrack for understanding spatial positions.

Importantly, participants reported that sound effects did not add mental burden; rather, they "add extra fun" (P1) and helped develop richer scene understanding. Twelve of 14 participants found the retrieved effects more helpful than the original soundtrack for understanding spatial positions. P7 noted that "as some of the objects did not make sound, adding retrieved sound effects gave me a feeling to walk close to that object and observe it. " Some participants reported confusion when different objects of the same category shared the same sound effect. -contrast color masks benefit low-vision users. Four participants with residual light or color perception confirmed the usefulness of the high-contrast overlay. P6 could "easily locate and identify where the object is in the frame," and P9 noted that the mask "doesn't require me to put extra attention is a good addition. " The feature reduces localization difficulty without adding cognitive burden, though its efficacy varies with vision condition.

section: Triggers and motivations for active exploration
content: [! htb]     Beyond feature-level feedback, we analyzed when and why participants chose to explore, revealing four patterns: 1: Original ADs anchor active exploration. 61. 4\ 2: Users pause for reflecting, validating, and customizing. Participants paused to reflect on complex scenes with simultaneous events (P4), to validate narrative coherence by navigating to adjacent frames ("The description was: `the two casts hugged. ' I was like, what happened before they hugged? " --- P9), and to customize their experience by skipping unengaging content (P3). 3: Frame description granularity affects object exploration. When frame descriptions were already detailed, users tended to skip object exploration ("the frame description felt self-explanatory" --- P1). Paradoxically, when frame descriptions were sparse, some users also skipped exploration, assuming the scene was uninteresting (P8). Users explored objects on 43. 8\ 4: New sounds and mentioned objects trigger exploration. 14.

section: Handling AI errors through contextual cross-checking
content: Since Spica's ML pipeline is imperfect, understanding how BLV users handle errors is essential. We found that participants could identify some errors by leveraging contextual information across Spica's layered descriptions. P5 initially felt "confused when the description told me there was a second person in the scene, but I only heard one person's sound. " By examining adjacent frames, he determined the description was likely incorrect. P13 demonstrated a different strategy, inferring errors from contextual mismatch: "When I heard there was an accordion as I explored I feel that's probably not, because the scene was a kitchen; maybe it was a cutting board. " When errors could not be resolved through context---for instance, when an object was neither mentioned in the frame description nor audible in the soundtrack---participants typically chose to skip the uncertain information and continue, treating it as an expected limitation of AI-assisted tools (P9).

section: Balancing Information Richness with Cognitive Load
content: A central challenge in designing AI-augmented multimodal systems is calibrating the volume and granularity of information the system provides. Users differ in their capacity and preference for how much detail they want, in what form, and through which modality. In the context of BLV video consumption, detailed audio descriptions help users understand visual content, yet increasing detail also raises the cognitive burden — particularly because BLV users rely predominantly on the auditory channel to process information that sighted users distribute across vision and hearing simultaneously. Recent work has highlighted the importance of reducing cognitive load while preserving key information in multimedia accessibility . In our study, users often preferred receiving feedback at the moment of a trigger event, yet conventional Audio Descriptions frequently omit or delay such feedback. Spica addresses this tension through layered captions: the base layer preserves the original audio description, while an optional layer surfaces AI-generated object captions on demand. This design reflects a broader principle for human-AI collaborative systems — rather than imposing a fixed level of detail, the system should support user agency in customizing the types, amount, and modalities of information they receive, allowing individuals to manage their own cognitive load.

section: Compensating for Missing Modalities through AI-Generated Sensory Channels
content: When a user lacks access to a primary modality, AI can compensate by translating information into alternative sensory channels. Spica demonstrates this principle in two ways. First, beyond verbal scene descriptions, Spica enriches the auditory experience by generating contextually relevant sound effects linked to detected objects, creating a deeper sense of immersion. Importantly, these sound effects are spatialized using calculated 3D object positions, enhancing users' perception of where objects are located within a scene. Participants broadly valued this feature, noting that auditory cues naturally augment video viewing. P9 further suggested that layering environmental sounds — such as actual street ambiance rather than isolated effects — could enhance realism even further. Future work could explore tighter alignment between sound effects and scene context, potentially leveraging advanced retrieval or sound synthesis models . Additionally, while Spica currently provides static spatial audio to indicate object position, incorporating temporal cues — allowing users to perceive object movement through shifting sound — represents a promising direction, as echoed by P5's suggestion that hearing a car's sound move through space would be more immersive. Second, Spica enables users to interact with visual scenes through touch, fostering an intuitive understanding of object positions and spatial relationships within a frame. This points toward a broader design space in which AI-augmented systems employ additional sensory channels — such as haptic feedback for conveying shape and texture — to compensate for missing visual information. Recent advances in computationally transforming 2D data into 3D environments further open possibilities for gestural and embodied interaction in VR-based video exploration.

A key design decision in human-AI collaborative systems is how users initiate and navigate information seeking. Spica supports both keyboard-based and touch-based interaction for object exploration. Drawing on multimodal interaction theory , the touch-based approach integrates tactile and auditory perception, allowing users to build an intuitive spatial mental model — the position of a user's finger maps directly to the relative location of an object in the scene. However, this approach risks users missing objects they do not physically encounter, and may become overwhelming in complex scenes with many objects. An alternative is a voice-based exploration strategy, where users verbally query the system about specific objects and receive audio descriptions in response . While this provides immediate, targeted feedback, it lacks discoverability — users cannot learn about objects they do not already know to ask about. Additionally, inaccuracies in voice recognition and intent classification can introduce frustration and misinformation . These tradeoffs suggest that effective human-AI collaborative systems should offer complementary interaction strategies, allowing users to fluidly switch between exploratory and targeted modes depending on their goals and context.

section: Adapting to Diverse Capabilities and Preferences of BLV Users
content: Finally, designing AI-augmented multimodal systems requires accommodating the spectrum of user capabilities rather than treating any user group as monolithic. In our study, we found that careful use of the visual modality can still benefit low-vision users who retain partial visual access. Spica uses high-contrast color masks to highlight selected objects, making visual information more salient for users with residual vision. This principle extends further: for users with color vision deficiencies such as Daltonism, adaptive systems could provide alternative color schemes tailored to individual vision conditions. Research on color cues for learning also suggests opportunities in the video domain — for example, using gradual shifts in color intensity to signal temporal changes in object states. More broadly, this underscores a design imperative for human-AI collaborative systems: AI should not only compensate for missing modalities but also amplify the capabilities users already have, adapting its output to each individual's cognitive and perceptual profile.

Spica demonstrates how AI can compensate for a missing perceptual channel while preserving user agency and adaptability. By layering AI-generated descriptions and spatial sound effects on top of existing audio descriptions, Spica transforms video consumption from a passive, one-size-fits-all experience into an interactive, user-directed exploration. The system addresses all three recurring challenges of this dissertation: it supports error handling by enabling contextual cross-checking across layered descriptions, so that users can discover and assess potential inaccuracies without an independent visual channel; it reduces cognitive load by giving users control over the timing, depth, and modality of information access, rather than forcing all visual content through a single narration stream; and it accommodates diverse user capabilities by offering multiple exploration techniques---touch, keyboard, spatial audio, and high-contrast overlays---that adapt to a wide range of vision conditions and personal preferences. The user study confirmed that Spica significantly improves both understanding and immersion over conventional audio descriptions, while revealing rich behavioral patterns in how BLV users exercise their newfound agency to explore video content.

section: Aroma: Mix-Initiative AI Assistance for Non-Visual Cooking
content: (This chapter is modified from the conference paper: Zheng Ning, Leyang Li, Daniel Killough, JooYoung Seo, Patrick Carrington, Yapeng Tian, Yuhang Zhao, Franklin Mingzhe Li, and Toby Jia-Jun Li. AROMA: Mixed-Initiative AI Assistance for Non-Visual Cooking by Grounding Multimodal Information Between Reality and Videos. In Proceedings of the 38th Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology (UIST '25), Busan, Republic of Korea, 2025.

The previous two chapters addressed settings in which AI augments a single user's perception of digital media: Mimosa (Chapter [ref]) blended AI into audiovisual creative cognition, while Spica (Chapter [ref]) compensated for missing visual perception during video consumption. This chapter expands from digital media to a real-world, physically situated task. Presenting Aroma (Aroma is an acronym for Augmenting Recipe Orchestration with Multimodal Assistance), a AI-powered system that incorporates visual information from a BLV user's personal device that assists them in performing activities of daily living.  Cooking is among the most cognitively demanding activities of daily living (ADLs). It requires continuous assessment of food state through multiple sensory channels, such as sight, touch, smell, taste, and sound, and tight coordination between procedural knowledge and real-time observation . For blind or low-vision (BLV) individuals, the absence of visual input makes this coordination substantially harder. BLV cooks must rely on non-visual cues, such as texture, aroma, sizzling sounds, and spatial memory, to track progress and make decisions . Yet instructional resources, especially video recipes, are designed predominantly for sighted viewers: instructors often rely on visual demonstrations without sufficient verbal detail, or use vague references like "this" or "that" without context . Prior work shows that BLV users are enthusiastic about video recipes for their rich auditory cues and professional expertise, but primarily consume them for entertainment rather than direct task guidance due to the lack of detailed visual descriptions . This disconnect highlights a fundamental challenge: bridging the gap between the non-visual sensory inputs that BLV users rely on and the audiovisual instructional content in video recipes.

Prior work shows that BLV users are enthusiastic about video recipes for their rich auditory cues and professional expertise, but primarily consume them for entertainment rather than direct task guidance due to the lack of detailed visual descriptions . This disconnect highlights a fundamental challenge: bridging the gap between the non-visual sensory inputs that BLV users rely on and the audiovisual instructional content in video recipes. Prior research has offered valuable design insights for AI systems that support BLV cooking , but a critical gap remains: enabling real-time collaboration between users and AI that leverages their complementary perceptual and cognitive strengths to align information from the physical environment with instructional knowledge in the video. These challenges directly instantiate the three recurring concerns of this dissertation. Error handling: cooking errors, such as a missed step or an incorrect ingredient, can be difficult for BLV users to detect without visual feedback, and AI models used for real-time assistance inevitably introduce their own errors, requiring mechanisms that keep users informed and in control. Cognitive load: BLV users must simultaneously manage procedural recall, sensory monitoring, and physical manipulation; referencing a lengthy video recipe during cooking further increases mental effort . Diverse user capabilities: BLV users vary widely in cooking experience, residual vision, and preferred interaction strategies, so the system must adapt to individual needs and contexts. Aroma addresses these challenges through a mixed-initiative architecture that pairs BLV users' in-situ non-visual perceptions with a multimodal AI agent grounded in video recipe content. Users can access procedural steps, verify cooking state, and receive targeted guidance by querying the system based on their own sensory observations.

Aroma addresses these challenges through a mixed-initiative architecture that pairs BLV users' in-situ non-visual perceptions with a multimodal AI agent grounded in video recipe content. Users can access procedural steps, verify cooking state, and receive targeted guidance by querying the system based on their own sensory observations. The system also continuously monitors the cooking environment through a wearable camera feed, proactively analyzing the scene and raising alerts when misalignments or errors are detected. This collaboration foregrounds error handling through proactive monitoring and corrective feedback, lowers cognitive load by letting the system track procedural state and provide timely guidance, and adapts to diverse capabilities by supporting flexible, conversational interaction that respects users' embodied expertise. The contributions of this chapter are: (i) Aroma, a mixed-initiative multimodal AI system that supports BLV users in cooking by aligning real-world sensory feedback with instructional video content; (ii) empirical evidence from a user study with eight BLV participants, demonstrating the system's usability and its impact on procedural understanding, autonomy, and error recovery; and (iii) design insights for building interactive AI systems that leverage complementary human-AI perceptions to support BLV individuals in performing ADLs.

To illustrate how Aroma supports the interplay between a BLV user's non-visual perceptions and AI-provided visual analysis during cooking, we present a scenario in which Jane, a congenitally blind user, uses Aroma to prepare Spaghetti Bolognese.

. Jane starts by watching a video recipe and listening to it from beginning to end. While this gives her a general idea of the steps, she cannot remember everything, and many details are unclear. She opens Aroma and loads the video. The system first processes the video content; it then uses Jane's mobile phone as a first-person camera to stream the kitchen environment in real time while capturing her voice commands for processing. 1: Boiling Pasta. Following the tutorial, Jane fills a pot with water and places it on the stove. The real-time monitoring agent aligns her actions with the reference from the tutorial video. It detects that Jane has forgotten to add salt. In response, the system proactively alerts her: "It appears you have not added salt to your boiling water. Adding salt enhances the flavor of the pasta. " 2: Saut\'eing Onions and Garlic. Jane recalls that her next step is to saut\'e chopped onions and garlic in olive oil. While preparing the ingredients, she feels uncertain about the knife technique, so she asks, "How do I properly chop onions? " The system responds with: "Onion should be chopped into small chunks," synthesized from the video content. Jane finds the response too general, so she tries chopping the onion, uses her sense of touch to judge the thickness, and follows up: "I'm chopping my onion this thin, is this correct? " The system replies, "According to the video recipe, the onions are chopped into thicker square slices. For making sauce, there's no need to chop them finely. " Satisfied, Jane continues. 3: Cooking the Sauce. After chopping the onions, Jane forgets what comes next, so she asks: "What's my next step?

3: Cooking the Sauce. After chopping the onions, Jane forgets what comes next, so she asks: "What's my next step? " The system considers the history of her previous actions, retrieves the content from the video, and responds: "Next, you should prepare the sauce. Heat oil in a pan, saut\'e the onions and garlic, and then incorporate tomato sauce and herbs. " Uncertain, Jane says "play the video recipe" to replay the relevant segment from the original video recipe. 4: Combining Pasta and Sauce. In the final step, Jane wants to reflect on a previous action: "Did I drain the pasta already or is it still in the water? " The system retrieves the answer from its stored memory of the session. This scenario illustrates Aroma's mixed-initiative workflow: the system responds to user-initiated queries grounded in non-visual perception while proactively monitoring the cooking process to detect and alert the user about errors. This approach reflects the dissertation's goals of supporting error handling through proactive monitoring, reducing cognitive load by offloading procedural tracking to the system, and accommodating diverse user capabilities through flexible, conversational interaction.

Drawing on prior empirical findings about non-visual cooking , we identified the following design goals for Aroma, each connected to the dissertation's recurring challenges: - DG1: Provide both on-demand and proactive support. The system should respond promptly to user-initiated requests while also proactively monitoring the cooking process to detect potential errors and offer corrective suggestions, recognizing that BLV users may not always be aware when a mistake occurs . This goal directly addresses the dissertation's concern with error handling: in a high-stakes physical task, timely detection and correction of errors is essential, yet BLV users may lack the visual feedback needed to identify mistakes independently. - DG2: Bridge the gap between real-world cooking states and video content. The system should fuse the user's non-visual perception, e. g. , "I can feel the dough is sticky," with real-time visual analysis, e. g. , "The dough is too thin compared to the recipe's demonstration," to align the user's actual cooking state with the intended recipe steps. This goal maps to the dissertation's concern with cognitive load: by combining complementary perceptual channels, the system reduces the mental effort users must expend to determine their current state relative to the recipe. - DG3: Support flexible and accessible interaction with video recipes. Referencing video recipes during cooking imposes high cognitive load due to their length and multimodal nature . The system should accommodate diverse user needs by offering multiple forms of support, such as concise step-by-step guidance, detailed explanations, and the ability to jump directly to relevant video segments. This goal relates to diverse user capabilities: users with different cooking experience and interaction preferences require different levels of detail and control.

Aroma operates through a mixed-initiative architecture that continuously integrates three information streams (Fig. [ref]): (i) non-visual perceptions (0. 6em): the BLV user's own sensory cues, such as smell, touch, and taste, conveyed verbally; (ii) visual information (0. 6em): real-time analysis of cooking states from a wearable first-person camera (Fig. [ref]); and (iii) video recipe knowledge (0. 6em): structured information extracted from the given video recipe. Four primary components (0. 6em in Fig. [ref]) coordinate these inputs. A video information extraction pipeline parses the recipe video into a structured JSON knowledge base covering spoken instructions, visual descriptions, and environmental sounds. A real-time visual analysis agent (implemented with a visual LLM) streams the wearable camera feed and monitors cooking states by aligning visual observations with the video content. An interactive conversational agent (implemented with another LLM) handles user requests based on their non-visual input, visual information from the visual agent, and the session context. A context management module maintains shared memory across both agents, storing conversational history and periodic visual analysis results, and dispatches relevant context for each response. [! htb]     To adapt responses to the preferred formats of BLV users identified in prior work , Aroma uses a state-machine framework (Fig. [ref]) that tracks different interaction states, e. g. , confirming a cooking state, describing an ingredient, or retrieving a previous step's information. Each state is associated with prompt templates that instruct the backend LLMs how to generate a response. Transitions occur either from user-initiated requests or from changes detected through real-time visual monitoring.

section: Contextualized Responses Grounded in Visual and Non-Visual Perception
content: Since cooking involves multimodal information where both visual and non-visual inputs are important for determining cooking state, Aroma integrates visual data captured through the wearable camera with non-visual information verbalized by the user. This combined perceptual input is then grounded in the instructional content from the video recipe (Fig. [ref]). Upon receiving a user query, the system determines the current event using the state-machine framework (Fig. [ref]) and transitions into one of four response categories: - Food State Responses (state 1) --- inquiries about the current condition of the food. - Step-Related Responses (state 2) --- clarifications regarding specific steps in the cooking process. - Problem-Solving Responses (state 3) --- assistance when encountering issues or uncertainties. - General Responses (state 4) --- broader inquiries related to the cooking procedure or recipe. Responses deliver concise, critical information relevant to the user's immediate needs. For example, if a user asks, "Is the chicken cooked through? ", the system provides a targeted response: "The chicken is lightly browned externally but requires additional cooking, as the internal temperature has not reached 165 F", deliberately omitting extraneous details to minimize cognitive load. If the user follows up for more detail (event 7) or indicates the previous response was wrong (event 8), the state transitions accordingly (states 5 or 6), and the backend LLM is prompted to pay special attention to the additional information provided by the user. The system adapts dynamically to non-visual perceptual input, such as information obtained through touch, sound, or smell, incorporating that context into more grounded responses.

section: Proactive Monitoring of the Cooking Process
content: Previous research highlights the increased cognitive load BLV users face when accessing recipe instructions during cooking, which often prevents them from noticing errors. To address this, Aroma adopts a mixed-initiative interaction model , blending user-initiated requests with system-initiated assistance. The visual analysis agent continuously monitors the cooking environment through the first-person camera. Every two seconds, it performs two operations: (i) generating objective observations of the current scene, and (ii) comparing those observations with reference knowledge from the video recipe. Specifically, the agent observes the cooking action being performed, the corresponding recipe step, visible food items and kitchenware, and identifiable cooking-related sounds. Based on these observations, it determines whether the activity is relevant to the recipe, whether the step is being executed correctly, whether any required steps have been missed, and whether the user has advanced to a new step. When the agent detects a deviation, such as a missed or incorrectly performed step, it alerts the user and provides corrective instructions. These situations correspond to events 5 and 6 in the state machine (Fig. [ref]), prompting a transition to a problem-solving state that triggers the appropriate response.

section: Accessing Video Segments and Stored Memory
content: To support DG3, Aroma enables users to access segments from the original instructional video as well as the memory of previous interactions and visual analysis results. Users can request specific video segments directly, e. g. , "Replay the part that tells me what ingredients I should prepare. " Alternatively, after receiving a natural-language response, they can issue commands like "play" or "pause" to hear the corresponding video clip. The design rationale is grounded in prior research showing that AI-provided concrete examples strengthen user trust , and it aligns with BLV users' common practice of navigating back to relevant parts of a recipe . To address the practical challenges of managing multi-step cooking tasks, the system also allows users to retrieve information from earlier stages of the session. Aroma maintains a comprehensive record of both the conversational history and the automatic visual analysis results. When the user makes a retrieval-related request, such as "Did I already add the garlic? ", the system synthesizes a response from the relevant portions of stored memory, enabling users to reflect on or resume prior steps without guesswork.

The system is implemented using Next. js. We use the OpenAI Real-time API to continuously transcribe users' voice commands and map them to events in the state-machine framework (Fig. [ref]). The real-time visual analysis agent is achieved by periodically making requests to the Multimodal Live API from Gemini. We use GPT-4o-mini to generate user-initiated responses (Sec. [ref]) and to extract relevant clips (Sec. [ref]) from the original video at the sentence level. Text-based responses are converted to audio through the OpenAI Text-to-Speech API. To extract multimodal information from video recipes, we use a set of AI models to obtain text-based descriptions from each modality separately. We first separate the vocal part from the video, typically containing spoken instructions, and store the transcribed text. We then parse the transcript at the sentence level and use PySceneDetect to extract key frames representing different visual scenes corresponding to each sentence's time interval. Next, we use GPT-4o to generate visual descriptions for each sentence by passing in all associated key frames, prompting the model to describe cooking steps, ingredient appearance, relative positions, and relationships between ingredients and kitchenware. We use GAMA to generate text-based descriptions for environmental sounds such as sizzling or simmering. All extracted information is compiled into a structured JSON file that serves as the system's multimodal knowledge base. Before the system starts running, the user can configure the speed of text-to-speech according to their preference. During runtime, the state machine automatically returns to the initial state when the user indicates satisfaction or when the system remains idle for a set period (five seconds in our implementation).

We evaluated Aroma through an in-kitchen user study with eight BLV participants (This study was approved by the IRB at our institution. ) to assess both system performance and user experience. The study addressed the following research questions: - RQ1:: How effective is Aroma in assisting BLV users during cooking? - RQ2:: How do BLV users perceive the agency, control, and helpfulness when collaborating with Aroma? - RQ3:: What is the mental process of the user when coordinating with complementary perceptions from the system?

We recruited eight BLV participants. Participants were screened using a demographic questionnaire to confirm that their visual acuity was worse than 20/200 and that they had no physical or medical conditions affecting mobility or the ability to handle kitchen tools. We did not impose strict screening criteria on prior cooking experience. The average age was 37. 1 ( = 13. 7). Five participants were female and three were male. Detailed demographic information is shown in Table [ref]. [! htb] c c c c X X X ID & Age & Gender & Onset & Level of Visual Impairment & Occupation & Cooking Frequency \\ P1 & 34 & M & Congenital & Blindness with some light/color perception & Professor & About once a week \\ P2 & 33 & F & Congenital & Total Blindness & Self-employed & About once a week \\ P3 & 33 & M & Congenital & Blindness with some light/color perception & Massage therapist & Almost every day \\ P4 & 25 & F & Congenital & Total Blindness & Student & Less than once a week \\ P5 & 62 & M & Acquired & Total Blindness & Chef & Almost every day \\ P6 & 28 & F & Congenital & Total blindness & Student & About once a week \\ P7 & 24 & F & Acquired & Blindness with some light/color perception & Student & Less than once a week \\

We conducted the study in participants' own kitchens (P3, P5, and P8) or at alternative locations of their choice. We prepared three video recipes (Table [ref]), and each participant freely chose one. These videos were preprocessed by Aroma's knowledge extraction pipeline. All ingredients were prepared in advance by the experimenter and placed on a table. For safety reasons, no heat-generating appliances or sharp knives were used during sessions. Steps involving heat were skipped. Each session lasted about one hour, and participants were compensated with a \50 USD gift card. After the consent process and a brief overview, the researcher informed the participant about the available recipes. The participant selected one, and the researcher demonstrated each system feature using a different recipe. Participants then listened to the full selected video recipe to develop a high-level understanding of the dish. Before cooking began, the researcher helped each participant wear a chest strap outfitted with a mobile phone that streamed a first-person video feed to the system. This chest-mounted setup was chosen based on prior findings showing it offers greater comfort and more stable footage than head-mounted alternatives . The participant then cooked the selected dish with the help of Aroma. After completing the cooking task, participants filled out a post-study questionnaire rating Aroma's usability, usefulness, and features on a 7-point Likert scale. A semi-structured interview lasting 10--20 minutes followed. We conducted a thematic analysis of the interview transcripts and observational notes, using open coding to identify user behaviors, perceptions, and preferences, iteratively grouping codes into broader themes through axial coding. Additionally, we annotated each study session recording, labeling user requests, events, and system responses.

We conducted a thematic analysis of the interview transcripts and observational notes, using open coding to identify user behaviors, perceptions, and preferences, iteratively grouping codes into broader themes through axial coding. Additionally, we annotated each study session recording, labeling user requests, events, and system responses. We evaluated Aroma on two aspects: (i) the accuracy of mapping each request to the correct event in the state machine (Fig. [ref]), computed as the proportion of correctly mapped user queries out of all queries excluding follow-ups; and (ii) the factual accuracy of the resulting response, computed as the proportion of correct answers out of all user queries. Two researchers independently annotated event categories and factual correctness as ground truth, discussing disagreements to reconcile differences.

Participants frequently issued requests to Aroma. Table [ref] reports the total request count, the system's event-mapping accuracy, and factual response accuracy. Across all participants, event-mapping accuracy averaged 0. 82, while factual accuracy averaged 0. 67. Most mapping errors stemmed from the diverse ways participants phrased the same type of request. For example, when users sought clarification on a specific step (event 2), some phrased it explicitly (e. g. , "Could you explain this step for me? "), while others used more colloquial language (e. g. , "What's going on now? "), occasionally causing misclassification. Although such misclassified replies could be factually accurate, they did not satisfy users' informational needs: as one participant reflected, "When I ask about a step, I expect details like how long it takes and how to perform it, but I remember once the system just simply told me what this step was. " Response errors most often arose from hallucinations in the underlying language model. As Aroma conditions each response on the runtime session history, accuracy degrades as the context window lengthens, a known limitation of LLM-based systems . This motivates future work on more efficient context management and retrieval algorithms. [! htb]     Overall, participants reported high satisfaction with Aroma's usability and effectiveness. Conversational features, including receiving immediate responses and asking follow-ups (Sec. [ref]), were rated as both highly important ( = 6. 13, = 0. 60) and useful ( = 6. 00, = 0. 50). System-initiated assistance (Sec. [ref]) received strong importance ( = 5. 88, = 0. 78) and usefulness ( = 5. 13, = 0. 78) ratings. The ability to replay video segments (Sec. [ref]) was rated lower ( = 4. 13, = 1. 36$ for importance), reflecting its role as a supplementary aid. Fig. [ref] summarizes the distribution of participant ratings.

Fig. [ref] summarizes the distribution of participant ratings. interaction supports flexible access to video recipes. Participants emphasized that conversational interaction allowed them to engage with content more flexibly, rather than being constrained by the linear structure of a video. P3 appreciated being able to re-ask questions: "I could ask what ingredients I need for the next step without hearing back from the video again. " As P1 noted: "With a video, once you miss something, it's hard to go back and catch it. " When system outputs were ambiguous, users followed up with contextual cues based on their own non-visual perception. For example, P5 first asked for an overview of the kitchen setup, then used that as a scaffold to ask specifically about the location of the pizza dough: "This helps me build a hierarchical understanding of what's on my counter. " Similarly, P4 used touch to distinguish between two plates when the system ambiguously referred to "a round plate": "I could feel there were two plates. So I picked one (from the texture) and asked, `Is this the pepperoni? ' and got confirmation. " feedback was helpful when precise and well-timed. Several participants found that proactive feedback helped them stay on track. P6 recalled, "The system automatically detected a wrong step where I was about to put the miso paste before adding the tofu into the soup. " P2 and P3 appreciated reminders to roll the pizza crust edge to cover the string cheese. These interventions reduced cognitive load, as P3 noted: "I know I have an assistant by my side to detect some, if not all, problems. " However, the timing and relevance of interventions posed challenges. Participants were frustrated when the system intervened during intentional deviations, and response latency sometimes caused misalignment with user actions. Despite these issues, precise proactive messages were generally well-received.

Participants were frustrated when the system intervened during intentional deviations, and response latency sometimes caused misalignment with user actions. Despite these issues, precise proactive messages were generally well-received. As P2 described, the system felt like a "background assistant" rather than a distraction. video replays complemented conversational guidance. Participants found this feature useful when the original audio content was accessible and informative. Replays helped users quickly "verify step sequences" (P6) or confirm specific actions. However, usefulness was limited for inaccessible video clips. P3 noted: "I tried this feature several times, but then I realized I couldn't get enough information I wanted, compared with other features.

section: What Requests Are BLV Users Making?
content: We analyzed the types of requests participants made throughout the cooking sessions, identifying key categories and their underlying motivations. awareness of procedural flow. Nearly all participants asked questions such as "What should I do next? " (P1) and "I have finished preparing the meat, what's the next step? " (P6). These requests were especially common after multiple prior requests in a step, or when participants wanted to skip a step, e. g. , "What should I do after baking the pizza? " (P2). types and locations of foods and ingredients. Participants often used the system to confirm identity or location of ingredients, especially when non-visual cues alone were insufficient. P4 asked "Is this the mozzarella? " when she felt a moist texture but could not distinguish it from pepperoni. P5 asked, "Do you see anything that looks like pizza dough? " These interactions illustrate a collaborative dynamic: participants brought non-visual sensory impressions while the AI contributed visual grounding, forming a hybrid perception loop. and response customization through follow-ups. Participants frequently followed up to clarify ambiguous instructions or tailor answers. P3 asked the system to "order it from top left to bottom right" after an initial scene description. P1 requested descriptions in a "clockwise direction. " P5 iterated multiple times about a 90-degree folding step: "Is this one the video said to rotate 90 degrees? Or am I doing it wrong? " These interactions emphasize the importance of supporting iterative, dialogic clarification, echoing findings that conversational agents often fail when they cannot negotiate meaning with users through follow-up exchanges .

section: How Non-Visual and Visual Perception Complement Each Other
content: Our study revealed how BLV users integrate visual assistance from the AI system into their existing non-visual cooking strategies. Rather than replacing their tactile, auditory, olfactory, and gustatory skills, Aroma acted as a complementary scaffold that enhanced these well-practiced abilities. -visual perception as the foundation for assessing food state. Experienced participants began with robust, non-visual schemas for gauging food readiness. P5, a blind chef, described how different bowl shapes help identify ingredients: "I use different shapes and sizes and textures. So if the computer couldn't answer me, I could. " P3 explained how he could "feel the edge of the pan so I know how far I need to stretch the dough. " These examples illustrate that many users used the AI primarily to validate their own perceptions, rather than depending on it as a primary information source. ambiguities in object and step identification. Participants frequently used Aroma to verify inferences made through touch or sound. P5 explained while handling pizza dough: "I think this is probably triangular... I can tell by touch, but I'm just confirming. " This interplay of tactile inference and visual confirmation was especially important for objects with similar textures. P2 struggled to distinguish between string cheese and mozzarella, noting that "they all have similar textures and flavors, and the video doesn't say what they look like either. " This finding directly parallels the mutual disambiguation paradigm from multimodal interaction theory : each modality resolves ambiguities that the other cannot. spatial awareness of ingredients and tools. Participants used the AI's egocentric vision to orient themselves within the physical workspace. Nearly every participant asked the system to help locate ingredients and confirm spatial arrangement. P2 asked, "Am I spreading the pizza sauce on the center of the dough?

section: How Non-Visual and Visual Perception Complement Each Other
content: Nearly every participant asked the system to help locate ingredients and confirm spatial arrangement. P2 asked, "Am I spreading the pizza sauce on the center of the dough? " P3 combined a physical demonstration with a spatial question: "If cheese sticks were evenly placed here? " while pointing to the pizza crust. expectations: extending visual perception to include gestures. Participants expressed preferences for personalized spatial descriptions: P6 requested "clockwise direction" descriptions, while P3 preferred hand-relative coordinates (e. g. , "left of your right hand"). These observations suggest two directions for future work: personalizing responses based on users' preferred spatial language, and incorporating gesture recognition to provide additional context for real-time interaction.

section: From Sensory Compensation to Human-AI Co-Reasoning
content: A recurring insight across this dissertation is that AI should not merely replace a missing modality but instead collaborate with the user's existing cognitive processes. In Aroma, users did not treat the system as a primary information source; rather, they drew on well-practiced non-visual skills, such as touch, spatial memory, and auditory judgment, and used AI-provided visual analysis to validate, refine, or extend their own inferences. P5, a blind professional chef, captured this dynamic: "I use different shapes and sizes and textures. So if the computer couldn't answer me, I could. " This pattern is consistent with the mutual disambiguation paradigm from multimodal interaction theory : the user's embodied perception and the system's visual perception each resolve ambiguities that the other cannot. This finding generalizes a principle that emerged in earlier chapters. In Spica (Chapter [ref]), users similarly exercised agency over what and when to explore, treating AI-generated descriptions as a supplementary layer rather than a replacement for their own auditory understanding. Aroma extends this principle from digital media into a physically situated task, where the stakes of error are higher and the user's embodied expertise is richer. The implication for human-AI collaborative systems is that AI should be designed as a co-reasoning partner, one that builds on the user's ongoing cognitive process rather than substituting for it. This aligns with Dourish's argument that technology is most effective when grounded in the body and lived practice .

section: Balancing System Proactivity with User Agency and Privacy
content: Aroma's mixed-initiative architecture combines user-initiated queries with system-initiated monitoring and alerts. Our study showed that proactive feedback was most effective when it was precise and well-timed: participants valued alerts about missed steps (e. g. , forgetting to add salt) and appreciated that the system served as a "background assistant" (P2). However, participants expressed frustration when the system intervened during intentional deviations from the recipe, such as skipping, reordering, or modifying steps based on personal preference or contextual constraints. This tension between proactivity and user autonomy has a direct bearing on the dissertation's concern with error handling. In a mixed-initiative system, distinguishing between genuine errors and deliberate adaptations is essential: treating every deviation as an error undermines trust, while failing to flag actual mistakes compromises safety. The implication is that future human-AI collaborative systems should support procedural fluidity rather than enforcing rigid adherence to predefined steps. This means modeling users' intent to differentiate errors from intentional modifications, and enabling configurable levels of proactivity so that users can specify when and how the system should intervene. In sensor-rich environments, this design challenge also intersects with privacy : continuous monitoring through wearable cameras raises concerns that must be addressed through consent-driven mechanisms aligned with individual expectations.

Aroma's current interaction paradigm relies on spoken commands and verbal descriptions of non-visual perceptions. However, during our study, participants naturally employed non-verbal cues throughout their cooking process: pointing at objects, tapping surfaces, and sweeping hand movements to indicate spatial queries. This observation is consistent with prior research on gesture-based non-visual interaction and connects to the dissertation's concern with cognitive load: verbalizing every sensory impression while simultaneously managing a multi-step physical task imposes substantial mental effort. The implication is that human-AI collaborative systems operating in physically situated contexts should expand their input channels beyond speech. Integrating gesture recognition would allow users to communicate spatial and referential information more naturally, reducing the verbalization burden. Beyond gestures with explicit semantic meaning, subtler behavioral signals, such as hesitation pauses, head movements, or micro-expressions , could serve as indicators of cognitive load, confusion, or uncertainty, enabling the system to adapt its response strategy proactively. Sensor augmentation, including thermal sensors, smart utensils, and audio classifiers , offers another path: by detecting cooking states directly, the system could reduce the need for users to verbalize their sensory observations, enabling a more seamless mixed-initiative flow.

section: Generalizing to Other Activities of Daily Living
content: While Aroma focuses on cooking, its core design principle, grounding a user's real-world sensory perception with instructional video content through mixed-initiative interaction, holds promise for other activities of daily living (ADLs). Tasks such as makeup application , home repair , arts and crafts , and gardening similarly involve multimodal perception, procedural knowledge, and embodied expertise. Each domain presents distinct sensory demands and interaction challenges, but the core approach of combining user-provided non-visual cues with real-time visual analysis and video-based instruction could transfer across domains. This connects to the dissertation's concern with diverse user capabilities: different ADLs place different demands on different modalities, so a generalizable framework must be flexible enough to accommodate varied perceptual profiles and task structures rather than assuming a fixed set of user needs.

Aroma demonstrates how AI can extend perceptual compensation from digital media consumption into a physically situated, real-world workflow. By pairing BLV users' non-visual perceptions with a multimodal agent grounded in video recipe content, Aroma addresses all three recurring challenges of this dissertation: it supports error handling through a mixed-initiative architecture that proactively monitors the cooking process, detects deviations such as missed or incorrectly performed steps, and alerts the user with corrective guidance; it reduces cognitive load by offloading procedural tracking, ingredient identification, and state comparison to the system, so that users can focus on the physical act of cooking rather than on recalling and cross-referencing video instructions; and it accommodates diverse user capabilities through conversational interaction that respects users' embodied expertise, allowing experienced cooks to use the AI primarily for validation while supporting less experienced users with step-by-step guidance.

Knowledge work depends on repurposing content across formats: a quarterly earnings report becomes a slide deck, a slide deck becomes a newsletter, a design specification becomes code and marketing copy. Although these artifacts share underlying ideas, they differ in modality (text, charts, images), format (bullet points versus prose), and level of abstraction (summary versus detailed analysis). Maintaining coherence across such heterogeneous representations is cognitively demanding. When one artifact changes, authors must manually locate corresponding passages in others, decide what should be updated, and rewrite content in a form appropriate to each representation. Errors in synchronization, such as mismatched numbers or outdated claims, can undermine credibility and disrupt collaboration . This chapter presents Transmogrifier, a technical probe to investigate dynamic transformations among representations with generative AI models in everyday knowledge work. Transmogrifier introduces interpretive linking, a generalization of the brushing-and-linking technique from information visualization to heterogeneous knowledge artifacts. In traditional coordinated multiple views, brushing highlights corresponding data points across views and linking propagates interactions such as filtering or selection. These techniques assume stable identifiers and deterministic correspondences. Knowledge-work artifacts rarely exhibit such structure; correspondences must instead be inferred semantically. Interpretive linking reframes linking as semantic coherence management. When content in one artifact changes, the system identifies semantically related elements in other artifacts and updates them while respecting each artifact's communicative role. Rather than enforcing exact equivalence, interpretive linking maintains consistency under ambiguity. This design foregrounds the three recurring challenges of this dissertation.

Rather than enforcing exact equivalence, interpretive linking maintains consistency under ambiguity. This design foregrounds the three recurring challenges of this dissertation. Error handling: cross-representation changes are made inspectable and revisable through visual traceability and version history, so users can verify and correct AI-mediated propagations. Cognitive load: the system coordinates edits across artifacts automatically, relieving authors from the burden of manually tracking and synchronizing related content. Diverse user capabilities: flexible, semantically grounded transformations accommodate different authoring practices, from direct content editing to meta-description specification, without requiring prompt-engineering expertise. Using a research-through-design methodology , we built a web-based technology probe that operationalizes interpretive linking across text, charts, and images, and conducted a qualitative study with six professional content creators. Participants generated 22 application concepts, ranging from responsive documents that maintain coherence across representations to collaborative ideation tools that support parallel work across modalities. The contributions of this chapter are: (i) the concept of interpretive linking, which extends brushing and linking to heterogeneous knowledge artifacts; (ii) a working technology probe that demonstrates how generative AI can support semantic propagation across text, charts, and images; (iii) a probe-to-prototype methodology that elicits grounded application scenarios from knowledge workers; and (iv) design considerations for human-centered systems that coordinate evolving multimodal artifacts.

To illustrate how interpretive linking supports the coordination of heterogeneous knowledge artifacts, we present three scenarios (Fig. [ref]): within a single artifact (S1), across multiple artifacts (S2), and in multimodal collaboration (S3). [! htb]    1: Coordinated Representations Within a Single Artifact. Allen is preparing a financial presentation. A slide contains a title, bullet points summarizing key figures, and a bar chart visualizing revenue. When Allen edits a revenue number in the bullet list, the chart updates accordingly. Later, he modifies a value directly in the chart. Interpretive linking propagates the semantic change back to the textual representations, revising claims such as "exceeded expectations" to "met expectations" when appropriate. The system does not merely mirror numbers; it reinterprets higher-level statements in light of updated data. 2: Derived Artifacts Across Documents. Allen must produce a detailed written report based on his slide deck. He links the report to the presentation. The system generates structured paragraphs from slide content while preserving communicative intent. When Allen refines the report by removing a subsection or clarifying an argument, the corresponding slides update accordingly. Here, interpretive linking supports the maintenance of derived artifacts, enabling live semantic relationships across documents tailored to different audiences. 3: Multimodal Collaborative Creation. Allen and his daughter co-create a children's story. Allen writes text on a laptop; his daughter draws illustrations on a tablet. When she adds glasses to a bunny illustration, the textual description updates to reflect the new detail. When Allen modifies narrative elements, illustrations adapt stylistically.

When she adds glasses to a bunny illustration, the textual description updates to reflect the new detail. When Allen modifies narrative elements, illustrations adapt stylistically. In this scenario, interpretive linking acts as a mediator across modalities and collaborators, allowing participants to work within their preferred medium while preserving shared coherence. These scenarios illustrate interpretive linking's three-phase capability of identifying semantically related content, propagating changes across representations, and preserving communicative intent, which mirrors the dissertation's broader concern with supporting human cognition across multimodal representations.

section: From Brushing and Linking to Interpretive Linking
content:
Brushing and linking is a foundational technique in information visualization that enables analysts to explore data across multiple coordinated views . Brushing allows a user to select or isolate data within one view; linking ensures that these interactions are dynamically reflected in all other views. Knowledge workers frequently perform a mental analogue of this process: reading a section of a report to write corresponding summary lines, or scanning a chart to verify that textual claims remain accurate . Extending brushing and linking to knowledge work poses two core challenges.

. The first challenge concerns identifying which elements across representations should be linked. In visualization, marks correspond to shared data items through explicit identifiers. In knowledge work, relationships between passages, summaries, figures, and captions are implicit, contextual, and many-to-many. Generative AI models can infer these relationships by identifying semantically related concepts across representations without extensive explicit specification.

. The second challenge concerns what should change once links are in place. In visualization, linking primarily propagates selection or filtering state while the underlying data remains unchanged. In knowledge work, propagation involves content changes: authors revise text, refine arguments, and update visuals as ideas evolve. This raises questions about how edits in one representation should be interpreted and reflected elsewhere while preserving semantic coherence. Interpretive linking addresses both challenges by treating identification and propagation as fluid and ambiguous processes. Relationships across representations are inferred rather than fixed, and changes do not follow predefined correspondences.

section: From Brushing and Linking to Interpretive Linking
content: Interpretive linking addresses both challenges by treating identification and propagation as fluid and ambiguous processes. Relationships across representations are inferred rather than fixed, and changes do not follow predefined correspondences. This introduces risks such as semantic drift or hallucinations from non-deterministic AI behavior, but also enables cross-modal and cross-register editing not possible with traditional, deterministic linking. Interpretive linking thus reframes the problem from preserving exact correspondence to developing strategies that maintain semantic coherence and communicative intent as content evolves.

Drawing on the challenges above, we identified three design strategies to manage semantic coherence in interpretive linking: - DS1: Meta-descriptions as soft constraints. Each content artifact carries a natural-language description of its communicative role (e. g. , "bullet points for a presentation," "watercolor children's illustration," "horizontal bar chart"). These meta-descriptions guide transformations and preserve format-specific intent without rigid coupling, allowing the system to maintain communicative properties during linking while keeping user specifications succinct. - DS2: Version history for minimal change. The transformation process incorporates not only current source content and the target's meta-description, but also previous versions of both source and target. This historical context allows the system to infer which aspects of the representation should be preserved and which should be updated, supporting minimal yet meaningful change propagation. For example, when a numerical value is modified in a chart, only the semantically corresponding portion of a linked text description is revised. - DS3: Visual traceability of change. When linking propagates a change, updates are surfaced locally: modified text segments are marked in bold or struck through; corresponding image or chart regions are highlighted. Additionally, brushing allows users to select text, chart elements, or image regions to reveal related elements in other artifacts. This traceability supports error handling by making propagated changes inspectable, and reduces cognitive load by helping users maintain mental connections across representations.

To explore interpretive linking in practice, we adopted a research-through-design approach and built a web-based, multi-user technology probe that enables authors to create, edit, and coordinate multiple content artifacts on an infinite canvas (Fig. [ref]). [!

The fundamental unit of interaction is a content block, in which users specify both the modality (text, chart, or image) and a free-form meta-description of the content. Users can create, resize, and spatially arrange multiple content blocks, which are all linked upon creation. The canvas functions as an environment of multiple simultaneous views, where each content block represents a distinct artifact with its own communicative intent (e. g. , summary, explanation, visual illustration). For text blocks, users can highlight text for brushing or directly edit content to trigger linking. For chart blocks, users can select chart elements for brushing and edit values to trigger linking. For image blocks, users can sketch with a digital pen to add new visual content (linking) or use a highlight brush to indicate regions of interest (brushing). To isolate questions about interpretive linking and semantic coherence, the probe adopts a simplifying assumption that all content blocks on the canvas are connected . Exploring richer, user-controlled linking structures is beyond the scope of this probe.

The system constructs prompts that incorporate the current source content, the previous source version, the previous target version, and the target's meta-description (Fig. [ref]). This structure allows the model to identify what changed and apply minimal semantic updates while preserving format constraints. -to-text (and text-to-chart). Meta-descriptions are incorporated directly into LLM calls as soft constraints. The pipeline also provides prior versions of both linked blocks, leveraging generative models' ability to reason about similarities and differences. For charts, the LLM generates HTML code that is rendered in the frontend. -to-image. Cross-modal text-to-image linking integrates additional image models. Through iterative development, we explored two strategies. An initial approach based on direct image generation frequently produced subtle but unintended visual changes. We refined this with a controlled pipeline that combines image segmentation and inpainting using a diffusion model (Fig. [ref]). When a change originates from a text block (e. g. , replacing "cat" with "elephant"), the system first analyzes the source image's visual style, then generates a prompt specifying the desired change while preserving that style. A segmentation model identifies the referenced object and produces a mask, and the diffusion model inpaints the new object within the masked region. In the reverse direction, when a user modifies an image with a digital pen, the marked image is processed to match the original style, a vision model analyzes the update, and the LLM revises the corresponding text description. -to-image. Edits between two visual blocks are routed through an LLM because paired images may differ substantially in pose, viewpoint, or depiction. When a user sketches on one image, the sketch is first transformed to match the original image style.

Edits between two visual blocks are routed through an LLM because paired images may differ substantially in pose, viewpoint, or depiction. When a user sketches on one image, the sketch is first transformed to match the original image style. The LLM then analyzes the modified image to infer the intended change and produces a textual description. A segmentation model identifies the corresponding region in the target image, and a diffusion model performs inpainting to render the new object while preserving the target's overall visual context.

The front-end of the technology probe was implemented in React (, with a Node. js ( backend to synchronize data across multiple users and devices. The computational pipeline utilizes GPT-4o ( (Azure OpenAI, model gpt-4o-2024-05-13) and Stable Diffusion (v1. 10. 1). The Stable Diffusion model runs on a local machine equipped with an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU and an Intel Core i9-14900K 3. 20 GHz CPU.

To explore how interpretive linking could enable new forms of coordination and repurposing in knowledge work, we conducted a qualitative study with six professional content creators. Rather than evaluating a single predefined application, our goal was to use interpretive linking as a generative lens for uncovering emerging workflows, breakdowns, and opportunities across knowledge work practices.

We selected participants (2M, 4F) from different professional backgrounds: P1 was a UX researcher on video games; P2, a graphics designer for company applications; P3, a content designer for GUI labels and messages; P4, a content designer for office suite notifications; P5, a project manager for a collaboration application; and P6, a technical artist of 3D content for mixed reality. Each participant took part in two online sessions conducted 5--7 days apart. We employed a probe-to-prototype approach in which workflow ideas were captured during a first ideation session (60 min), materialized by the research team using the probe between sessions, and returned to participants for reflection in a second session (30 min). The first session began with a brief study overview, consent, and demographic collection. Participants were then introduced to the technology probe through a short video and live demonstration, with opportunities to ask questions. Next, the experimenter presented three example concept proposals built with the probe, eliciting feedback and prompting participants to propose workflow ideas grounded in their own practices. Between sessions, the research team rapidly prototyped participants' proposed workflows using the technology probe. When specific functionalities or modalities were not supported (e. g. , video), we created closely related variants. Given time constraints, two of the three proposed scenarios per participant were selected for the second session, prioritizing diversity across workflows. The second session focused on reflection. The experimenter demonstrated the two participant-proposed prototypes and gathered design considerations for each, structured around application setup, coordination patterns (e. g. , when and how changes should propagate), and coordination mechanisms (e. g. , whether propagation should be automatic or on-demand).

g. , whether propagation should be automatic or on-demand). Materializing abstract ideas into low-fidelity interactive prototypes enabled participants to concretely assess opportunities, constraints, and breakdowns, and to provide informed feedback on the underlying generative AI pipeline by engaging directly with the probe's behaviors in practice.

Across the study, participants proposed 22 workflow concepts that illustrate how interpretive linking can unlock new opportunities for knowledge work (Fig. [ref] shows representative examples). Participants' discussions around derived artifacts reflected common repurposing practices where the same material is adapted across representations, audiences, and contexts. Several participants suggested that interpretive linking could help coordinate these related representations, which are otherwise maintained through manual rewriting. P1 described transforming a research report into presentation-oriented artifacts, while P3 and P4 discussed rewriting content such as feature descriptions or notifications from different perspectives, including technical and non-technical audiences. Participants also noted that derived artifacts often serve different cognitive or communicative roles, such as P6's proposal of a learning playground for experimenting with unfamiliar programming languages by viewing code side by side with a familiar one. P1 also described the idea of derived artifacts in the form of filtered video transcripts, where user study recordings could be automatically segmented by topic (e. g. , a compilation of participant comments on game strengths versus frustrations). Beyond linking across artifacts, P4 identified opportunities for interpretive linking within a single artifact, proposing its use as a consistency checker between slide content and speaker notes to ensure alignment between on-screen points, narrative flow, and animation cues. and ideation. P1 and P3 discussed capturing brief notes on mobile devices during meetings or while away from their desks and later transforming these fragments into slides or visual materials, treating ideation and production as connected rather than fully separate stages. P2 suggested a related but distinct context inspired by the co-writing scenario in the TV series Westworld: an interactive story shaper in which a user specifies genre and format, and interpretive linking progressively shapes the narrative as the user iterates on a set of ideas. Participants reflected that interpretive linking helped reframe their thinking through multiple modalities, supporting reflection and perspective switching. P5 reflected that "you could see many different ways of seeing the same information. That was really cool," praising the value of multiple representations for stimulating creativity. P1 valued how interpretive linking made content feel like "seeing text brought to life," especially when creating alternative representations would otherwise require substantial manual effort. Others reflected on multi-perspective ideation, such as artists iterating on shared character concepts through different visual styles (P6), or interdisciplinary teams working across narrative text, storyboards, and scripts (P1). P2 raised accessibility as a compelling collaborative use case, describing how interpretive linking could generate sign language representations from speech during meetings, noting that "we currently pay a lot of money for interpreters" and that reliable automated translation would be transformative for team members who are deaf or hard of hearing. Several participants framed interpretive linking as a mediating layer in collaboration. P3 described the system as an "AI collaborator," and P4 characterized it as a mediator that could "transform what I have in my head into something others can consume. " P1 and P5 found these collaborative scenarios particularly compelling, with P5 envisioning writers and designers working on the same content through different representations, calling this "a cool, different human--AI interaction paradigm. " Together, these accounts suggest that interpretive linking enables collaborators to remain loosely aligned while contributing through representations suited to their roles, expertise, and communicative needs. Beyond leveraging interpretive linking within specific applications, participants also envisioned surfacing it as a general-purpose capability across tools. P1 proposed a smart copy-and-paste utility, noting that "I'm usually taking content from one type of application and moving it into another" and that restructuring and reformatting content was tedious. P2 described a smart linking feature between pieces of content that could "become a standard feature" in a wide range of applications, where users would add semantically aware links controlling source, target, and directionality between containers.

concentrates in hard or cascading changes. Participants consistently framed the value of interpretive linking in relation to the effort and risk of coordinating consequential changes across artifacts. Interpretive linking was seen as most compelling when updates would otherwise be time-consuming or error-prone to perform manually. As P1 noted, "Generative AI is better than me at making these kinds of changes---not small edits, but taking storyboards and turning them into a report. That would save hours. " This framing positioned interpretive linking less as automating trivial edits and more as supporting changes with cascading effects. Maintaining consistency across representations emerged as a key benefit. P5 noted that small discrepancies can undermine credibility: "there's nothing worse than when a number on a slide doesn't match the number in the chart. " P5 also reflected that "a small change, like modifying a single number, might have broader analytical implications," suggesting that AI could help surface these deeper implications. P4 highlighted scenarios where a single change impacts many documents, files, or collaborators, noting the difficulty of coordinating across an entire team's document set. At the same time, participants recognized that propagating changes automatically introduces new burdens. P2 reflected that tracking changes with cascading effects could shift the burden from making changes to verifying them. P5 noted that in collaborative workflows, automatic propagation without preview could be "jarring for other users," potentially disrupting the content creation process. These tensions highlight that the value of interpretive linking is not unconditional but depends on appropriate mechanisms for controlling scope, timing, and visibility of propagation. different interaction paradigm with AI.

These tensions highlight that the value of interpretive linking is not unconditional but depends on appropriate mechanisms for controlling scope, timing, and visibility of propagation. different interaction paradigm with AI. Participants highlighted how interpretive linking suggests a different mode of engaging with generative AI compared to chat-based prompting. P4 and P6 both noted a reduced need for explicit prompts, with P4 observing that "prompting is huge [difficult]---people don't know what to write. " Instead, interaction shifted toward specifying desired outcomes and directly editing AI-generated content. P4 described this as a "break in the existing mental model of how to communicate with LLMs," emphasizing the value of being able to "edit right away and focus on the content, not on how to talk to the system. " This positions interpretive linking as a mediator for coordinating meaning across representations, supporting reflection, collaboration, and iterative sensemaking with generative AI rather than isolated prompt-response exchanges. and change tracking. Participants consistently emphasized that propagation must be previewable and traceable. P3 noted that "even a small failure rate would massively affect my confidence in the system," reflecting that the non-deterministic nature of AI models makes even occasional errors disproportionately costly to user trust. P2 further cautioned that for high-stakes domains such as accessibility, unreliable capabilities could have serious implications, noting that "it's maybe one to steer clear from until the technology can catch up.

P2 further cautioned that for high-stakes domains such as accessibility, unreliable capabilities could have serious implications, noting that "it's maybe one to steer clear from until the technology can catch up.
