When a financial analysis moves from a prose report to a bar chart to a set of bullet points, the claims, the relationships among variables, and the supporting evidence all persist. What changes is the communicative form: modality, level of abstraction, rhetorical framing. Beneath every representation lies what we call the semantic substrate: the relational structure of meaning that remains invariant no matter which surface form it takes. The substrate is not itself a representation. It is what all representations of the same knowledge share. This distinction between substrate and surface form is not new; it recurs across intellectual traditions whenever a field confronts the puzzle of how the same meaning can wear different faces. Chomsky's generative grammar showed that diverse human languages conceal a shared deep structure: surface forms vary enormously, but the syntactic operations that generate them are universal . Peirce's semiotics established that meaning arises from the triadic relationship between sign, object, and interpretant, not from any particular sign itself . Gentner's structure-mapping theory revealed that analogical reasoning works by detecting relational parallels across domains whose surface features share nothing in common . The natural sciences follow the same logic: Newtonian mechanics unified terrestrial and celestial motion, Maxwell's equations revealed electricity and magnetism as one field, Darwin's natural selection explained both the diversity and unity of life. In each case, progress came not from describing new phenomena but from recognizing a shared substrate behind apparent diversity. What Transmogrifier contributes is a shift from observing this substrate to designing around it. Traditional knowledge tools treat each artifact as a self-contained object: a report is a report, a slide deck is a slide deck, and the burden of maintaining consistency falls on the author's memory and discipline.

What Transmogrifier contributes is a shift from observing this substrate to designing around it. Traditional knowledge tools treat each artifact as a self-contained object: a report is a report, a slide deck is a slide deck, and the burden of maintaining consistency falls on the author's memory and discipline. Interpretive linking inverts this relationship. It treats the semantic substrate as the organizing center and treats individual representations as projections of that center into different communicative forms, each tailored to a particular audience, modality, or cognitive purpose. The substrate becomes the first-class entity; representations become views. This reframing explains why switching between representations is cognitively valuable. Larkin and Simon showed that different representations of the same problem, while logically equivalent, are computationally non-equivalent: a diagram can make certain inferences trivial that would require extensive search in sentential form . Each representation selectively externalizes different facets of the substrate. The cognitive value of multiple representations is not redundancy but complementarity: each view reveals aspects of the substrate that others conceal. Our participants experienced exactly this when they described seeing the same content through multiple lenses as stimulating reflection and surfacing overlooked implications.

section: Generative AI as the Coordination Mechanism
content: Identifying a semantic substrate is one thing; maintaining it computationally across evolving, heterogeneous representations is another. This is where generative AI enters, not as a content generator, but as the mechanism that operationalizes the substrate. Modern large language models are well suited to this role because of how they are built. In machine learning, representation learning refers to the automatic discovery of internal features that capture structural regularities useful for downstream tasks . Through training on vast corpora spanning genres, formats, and registers, large language models have internalized the patterns by which the same meaning appears in different surface forms. They have, in effect, learned a computational approximation of the semantic substrate itself, which is what enables them to mediate between representations: detecting what changed, inferring which structural elements are affected, and propagating updates in a form appropriate to each target view. Cognitive science confirms that this coordination is where the leverage lies. Zhang and Norman showed that external representations are active participants in cognitive processing, fundamentally shaping which operations are easy and which are effortful . Ainsworth's DeFT framework demonstrated that multiple linked representations support deeper understanding by enabling richer mental models, with each representation contributing a distinct angle on the same underlying structure . Hutchins' distributed cognition framework generalized the point: cognition extends beyond the individual mind into the system of people, tools, and representations that surround it . The implication is that the quality of cognition depends on the quality of coordination among representations. Better coordination means better thinking. Transmogrifier's three design strategies address this coordination directly.

section: Generative AI as the Coordination Mechanism
content: Better coordination means better thinking. Transmogrifier's three design strategies address this coordination directly. Meta-descriptions (DS1) define each representation's communicative role, so propagation respects function rather than enforcing surface equivalence. Version history (DS2) distinguishes what has changed from what should be preserved, maintaining the substrate without overwriting intent. Visual traceability (DS3) makes the coordination inspectable, giving users the verification capacity that trust in automated mediation requires. Our participants' responses confirmed the value: they prized interpretive linking most for cascading changes where manual coordination would be time-consuming and error-prone, and they insisted on mechanisms for inspection, versioning, and selective acceptance.

If the semantic substrate is the organizing center and generative AI is the coordination mechanism, what changes for the user? The answer is a fundamentally different interaction paradigm, one in which people work through representations rather than on documents. In conventional workflows, users interact with individual artifacts: editing a paragraph, adjusting a chart, revising a slide. Each artifact is an island, and maintaining coherence across artifacts is a manual, cognitively demanding task. Interpretive linking collapses this fragmentation. Because all representations are projections of a shared substrate, editing any one of them is implicitly an edit to the substrate, which then propagates to the others. The user works in whichever representational form best fits the task at hand, and the system ensures that the rest of the ecosystem stays coherent. As P4 put it, this is a "break in the existing mental model of how to communicate with LLMs": rather than formulating prompts in a chat window, users edit content directly and let the substrate-level coordination happen behind the scenes. This paradigm has historical precedent. Bush's Memex envisioned associative trails connecting records, anticipating that the value of information lies in connections rather than in isolated documents . Nelson's hypertext formalized bidirectional links, establishing linking as a structural primitive for organizing knowledge . Engelbart's augmentation framework positioned linked information environments as extensions of human intellect . Each expanded what was thinkable by expanding what was linkable. Interpretive linking extends this trajectory from linking static documents to linking dynamic, multimodal representations that share a living substrate. The practical implication is a shift from AI as content generator to AI as coherence infrastructure.

Interpretive linking extends this trajectory from linking static documents to linking dynamic, multimodal representations that share a living substrate. The practical implication is a shift from AI as content generator to AI as coherence infrastructure. Knowledge workers today spend substantial effort on what amounts to manual substrate maintenance: updating slide decks when reports change, revising figures when data is updated, reformatting content for different audiences. Interpretive linking automates the structural coordination while keeping humans in control of the semantic decisions that require judgment. As generative models improve, this coordination will extend to richer modalities (video, audio, spatial environments), larger artifact ecosystems (entire project repositories rather than individual documents), and more nuanced forms of collaboration (resolving ambiguity across collaborators, adapting to audience-specific communicative norms, negotiating conflicting updates). The endpoint is an environment in which knowledge lives not in isolated documents but in networks of dynamically linked representations, each tailored to a specific cognitive or communicative purpose, all held together by AI-maintained coherence at the substrate level.

Transmogrifier demonstrates how generative AI can coordinate evolving representations in knowledge work through interpretive linking. By generalizing brushing and linking beyond structured data to heterogeneous knowledge artifacts, interpretive linking enables fluid transformation, collaboration, and reflection across formats and modalities. The system addresses all three recurring challenges of this dissertation: it supports error handling by making cross-representation changes inspectable through visual traceability and version history, reduces cognitive load by automating the coordination of edits across artifacts, and accommodates diverse user capabilities by embedding AI within direct content editing rather than requiring prompt-engineering expertise. The qualitative study with six professional content creators confirmed that interpretive linking is valued for cascading and structurally complex changes, and revealed that its utility extends beyond efficiency gains to enabling new forms of reflection, ideation, and collaboration. Participants envisioned interpretive linking as a general-purpose coordination layer that could be embedded across tools and workflows, from smart copy-and-paste utilities to cross-application semantic linking. At the same time, the study surfaced critical design tensions around propagation scope, user control, trust, and cross-modal predictability that must be addressed for interpretive linking to be adopted in practice. Together, these findings illuminate a rich design space in which AI-mediated coordination can support how knowledge workers create, adapt, and collaborate across multimodal representations.

Across the four systems, the size of user studies ranged from six to fifteen participants. While these sample sizes are consistent with formative HCI research that seeks qualitative insight rather than statistical generalization, they constrain how broadly we can claim the findings hold. The participant pools, although carefully recruited, represent only a fraction of the potential user populations. A related concern is the experiment settings. All four studies were conducted in controlled or semi-controlled settings with a limited set of tasks and time-bounded sessions. In each case, the gap between study conditions and everyday use means that some failure modes, adoption barriers, and emergent behaviors likely remain unobserved.

The systems presented in this dissertation are research prototypes designed to demonstrate feasibility and served often as probes to elicit design implications of the proposed interactive strategies, that being said, they are not tested the robustness, latency, and cost required for sustained, real-world deployment. Specifically, Mimosa's pipeline depends on the coverage of its sound separation, audio tagging, and object detection models. Spica's description generation relies on off-the-shelf vision and language models. While an offline technical evaluation validated their performance, the generated descriptions occasionally fail to integrate naturally with the video's original soundtrack, producing perceptual discontinuities for BLV users. The system also uses a fixed narration style and description structure, which may not suit the heterogeneous preferences observed among participants. Similarly, Aroma faces latency constraints that directly affect usability and safety. Despite leveraging multimodal APIs and real-time audio transcription to minimize delay, system-initiated prompts such as cooking-state analysis sometimes lagged behind user actions by several seconds, leading to situations where the system confirmed a step that the user had already completed. For Transmogrifier, its change-propagation pipelines revealed fundamental asymmetries across modalities. Text-to-text transformations were relatively reliable, but text-to-image propagation often introduced unintended visual changes or reinterpretations. More constrained pipelines using image segmentation and inpainting increased predictability but also limited by the model performance.

A recurring finding across the systems proposed in this dissertation is that the systems fail in ways that are difficult for users to anticipate, and that these failures compound in multi-step pipelines. In Mimosa, an error in object detection propagates to depth estimation, which propagates to spatial positioning, which propagates to the final audio output. Each stage amplifies or transforms upstream mistakes, making the source of a perceived error difficult to trace. In Spica, an incorrect object identification produces a misleading caption and an inappropriate sound effect, both of which the user may accept as accurate if they lack independent means of verification. In Aroma, a misidentified cooking state can trigger an erroneous proactive alert that interrupts the user's workflow and erodes trust. In Transmogrifier, a misinterpretation during change propagation can silently alter the communicative intent of a linked artifact. Although each system includes mechanisms to surface model outputs for user inspection, the effectiveness of these mechanisms varies with user expertise, task urgency, and the modality of the output. This suggests that error-handling mechanisms in multimodal AI systems cannot be designed in a modality-agnostic way; they need to account for the specific perceptual affordances and limitations of each output channel.

section: Final Discussion and Future Work: Rethinking Man-Machine Symbiosis in the Era of Multimodal Generative AI
content: HCI research has evolved across decades. Its early pioneers laid the conceptual groundwork: Engelbart's vision of augmenting human intellect and his landmark 1968 demonstration: "The Mother of All Demos" , Sutherland's Sketchpad which established the foundational concepts of graphical interaction, and Kay and Goldberg's Dynabook which imagined personal, dynamic computing as a medium for thought. These visions gave way to recent theoretical and application achievements such as the notable direct manipulation paradigm , in which humans pointed, clicked, and typed while machines displayed and computed, and strongly shaped the UX theories on interaction design . In the new century, application- and system-centric research proliferated, system integrates machine intelligence through symbolic AI and statistical learning that enabled classification, prediction, and recommendation, giving rise to human-in-the-loop systems.  Fortunately, the period in which this dissertation was written marks a transitional moment in AI community: the shift from conventional machine learning toward the generative AI paradigm, one will presumably effect on human life and work for decades. This era is grounded in foundational work in AI community including the Transformer architecture , instruction-tuned language models , multimodal systems , and the emerging agentic paradigm . These advances unlock capabilities that no prior generation of models could match: machines are close to, if not already capable of, perceiving, reasoning, and generating content fluidly across representational boundaries. As AI agents grow increasingly capable, exhibiting stronger reasoning, broader knowledge, and greater autonomy, what should the new human-AI collaboration paradigm look like? Cycling back to Licklider's foundational vision of man-computer symbiosis , the classical frameworks remain valid, yet they demand reinterpretation.

section: Final Discussion and Future Work: Rethinking Man-Machine Symbiosis in the Era of Multimodal Generative AI
content: As AI agents grow increasingly capable, exhibiting stronger reasoning, broader knowledge, and greater autonomy, what should the new human-AI collaboration paradigm look like? Cycling back to Licklider's foundational vision of man-computer symbiosis , the classical frameworks remain valid, yet they demand reinterpretation. In what follows, I offer my reflections on what a renewed symbiosis paradigm might look like, grounded in the four systems developed throughout this dissertation.

section: Building the Brain and the Nervous System
content: Building such symbiosis in the generative AI era requires efforts on two fronts: developing the brain, the AI agents themselves, and the nervous system, the interaction infrastructure that connects human and machine cognition.

section: Building AI's Brain: the Cognitive Engines of the Man-Computer Symbiosis
content: Today, generative models serve as a powerful cognitive engine for human-AI symbiosis. Theses models are able to operate across audio and visual modalities, which are the two most important ones for human intelligence. Specifically, large language models reason and communicate in text , multimodal models extend this capacity to vision channel, processing and generating content that mirrors how humans represent ideas visually . Beyond perception, on going research aims to internalize the structure of the world itself, i. e. the world model: LeCun proposes Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures as a path toward machine intelligence grounded in high-level, modality-agnostic world representations, while Ha and Schmidhuber demonstrate that agents can learn compressed spatiotemporal world models and use them to reason inside hallucinated simulations. Taken together, these advances point toward a common trajectory: AI systems that do not merely process inputs and emit outputs, but build rich internal representations of the world that increasingly overlap with the cognitive structures humans use to perceive, reason, and act. It is precisely this representational alignment across modalities that makes genuine symbiosis possible.

section: Building the Nervous System: The Coupling Layer for Human-AI Symbiosis
content: However, advances in the AI brain alone are insufficient to build a complete symbiosis. Like any organism, the system also requires a nervous system, not one internal to the AI agent, but the multimodal coupling layer between AI capabilities and human cognition. It is this interface layer that ultimately determines whether a powerful brain translates into the real-world of human's. The four systems in this dissertation are prototypes of this collaborative nervous system. Mimosa coupled an AI pipeline with human judgment through inspectable intermediate stages. Spica coupled AI-generated descriptions with BLV users' non-visual perception through layered, multi-granularity access from an interface that supports both restricted navigation through keyboards and touch. Aroma coupled AI visual sensing and reasoning capability of LLMs with the user's embodied expertise through a mixed-initiative interface. Transmogrifier coupled AI generative transformation with human editorial coherence through semantic substrate tracking. In each case, the design contribution was about leveraging the smart brain from AI to build a better collaborative nervous system. Through the research in this dissertation, we found that two key factors in building such a nervous system are error-handling and leveraging feedback. The first factor, error-handling, is necessitated by the fundamental imperfection of AI models. Large language and multimodal models hallucinate, produce locally coherent but globally inconsistent outputs, and fail unpredictably. These are not engineering defects awaiting a fix but consequences of the data-driven training process underlying these models. The second factor, leveraging feedback, follows directly from this imperfection: the user's ongoing perception, domain expertise, and contextual awareness function as continuous alignment signals that compensate where models fall short.

section: Building the Nervous System: The Coupling Layer for Human-AI Symbiosis
content: These are not engineering defects awaiting a fix but consequences of the data-driven training process underlying these models. The second factor, leveraging feedback, follows directly from this imperfection: the user's ongoing perception, domain expertise, and contextual awareness function as continuous alignment signals that compensate where models fall short. When model errors are exposed through appropriate modalities, the human grounds the AI by catching failures the model cannot detect in itself; simultaneously, the AI grounds the human by surfacing patterns and content beyond unaided perception. The practical implication is a shift from optimizing model autonomy to optimizing the quality of human-AI coupling. Both the brain and the nervous system are essential; neither can be optimized independently. Engelbart foresaw this six decades ago: not smarter machines alone but a "co-evolution of human capability and tool capability.

section: Towards the New Symbiosis in a Data-Driven and Human-Centered Way
content: To build the new symbiosis requires progress along two complementary axes: a data-driven axis concerned with improving the intelligence of AI models, and a human-centered axis concerned with cognition-grounded interaction design and rigorous evaluation of the coupling layer itself.

section: Unlock Personalized Model Intelligence in a Data-Driven Way
content: Data is the substrate of machine intelligence: the success of today's generative models rests on the scale of text, visual, and audio information on the internet . Yet data from the internet represents only a portion of processed, externalized knowledge of human society. Our internal thought traces are a far richer source of data, i. e. how we think, and how our brain processes the multimodal signals from the real world, one that captures personalized intelligence and holds potential for advancing general knowledge of AI models.  Specifically, current training paradigms capture what environments look like and what people have written about them, but not what an environment affords for a situated, embodied agent. Aroma made this gap concrete. Its BLV participants possessed cooking expertise that was fundamentally embodied, while the objective knowledge of recipes and cooking steps serves as the grounding. As noticed by study participants: P5 distinguished ingredients by shape and texture; P4 integrated tactile moisture cues with AI-mediated visual confirmation. No recipe text, however detailed, encodes what it feels like for dough to reach the right consistency, or how the acoustic signature of oil changes with temperature. Similarly, every interaction in which a user corrects a model's spatial positioning (Mimosa), rejects a fabricated description (Spica), overrides a AI-generated cooking-state judgment (Aroma), or refines a cross-modal propagation (Transmogrifier) produces a multimodal trace of human judgment that reflects their personalized mental model. These traces encode not just what the correct answer was, but how the human arrived at it, leveraging such personalized data could be a next for foundational models to move to situated, human-grounded intelligence.

The complementary axis concerns evaluation and design grounded in the user rather than benchmark datasets only. Current AI evaluation practice is overwhelmingly data-centric: large language models are ranked by accuracy on knowledge benchmarks such as MMLU , code-generation agents are scored by pass rates on HumanEval and issue-resolution rates on SWE-bench , and crowdsourced preference leaderboards such as Chatbot Arena . These benchmarks measure what a model can produce statistically, while omitting the subjective experience and contextual judgment of user perception. The four systems in this dissertation repeatedly exposed this gap. Models used in Spica to generate audio descriptions scored reasonably well, yet user studies revealed that temporal alignment and personalized granularity control mattered far more to BLV viewers. Transmogrifier's cross-modal propagation could not be meaningfully assessed by any existing automated metric, because correctness depended on whether the change preserved the user's communicative intent, a judgment only the user could render. In both cases, user-facing quality diverged from data-facing quality, and optimizing one did not guarantee the other. This observation is not new; it extends a long-standing commitment in HCI. Card, Moran, and Newell established that interface quality must be measured in terms of human cognitive and motor performance, not system throughput alone. Nielsen and Molich formalized heuristic evaluation as a means of surfacing usability failures that quantitative metrics miss. The challenge now is to extend this tradition to generative AI systems where the output space is open-ended, failure modes are subtle and context-dependent, and the human's role shifts from operator to collaborator. Concretely, this extension calls for evaluation benchmarks that are bottom-up rather than top-down. A top-down benchmark starts from model capabilities and asks whether users can leverage them.

Concretely, this extension calls for evaluation benchmarks that are bottom-up rather than top-down. A top-down benchmark starts from model capabilities and asks whether users can leverage them. A bottom-up benchmark starts from situated use cases: from the cook who needs to know whether the onions have caramelized, the BLV viewer who needs to follow a plot across scene transitions, the knowledge worker who needs to maintain argument coherence across a slide deck and a manuscript. It then asks what cognitive, perceptual, and collaborative demands these tasks impose, and designs instruments that measure constructs at the point of use: cognitive load , trust calibration, error recoverability, sense of agency, and the user's ability to maintain a coherent mental model of what the AI is doing and why. Evaluation of human-AI systems must account for this distribution, not merely the fidelity of the AI's external output. In practical terms, this means that a benchmark for a system like Aroma should not just ask whether the model correctly identified the cooking state, but whether the user received that judgment in a form, at a time, and through a channel that allowed them to act on it before the food burned. Finally, based on the leanings from the presented systems, theory-grounded interface design, which requires drawing on cross-disciplinary frameworks, effectively complement the empirical findings of data-driven experiments and user studies. These theories such as Gibson's ecological psychology frames perception as active affordance pickup; cognitive load theory constrains how information can be presented before overwhelming working memory; dual coding theory explains when multimodal presentation aids comprehension; and Norman's gulfs of execution and evaluation predict where opaque AI systems will break down for users.

Finally, based on the leanings from the presented systems, theory-grounded interface design, which requires drawing on cross-disciplinary frameworks, effectively complement the empirical findings of data-driven experiments and user studies. These theories such as Gibson's ecological psychology frames perception as active affordance pickup; cognitive load theory constrains how information can be presented before overwhelming working memory; dual coding theory explains when multimodal presentation aids comprehension; and Norman's gulfs of execution and evaluation predict where opaque AI systems will break down for users. These frameworks are not supplements to data-driven development, they are the cross-disciplinary foundation from which principled interface design should draw. Experimental results tell us what works; theory tells us why, and guides us toward designs that human minds can effectively use.

This dissertation investigated how AI-enabled interactive systems can augment human cognition in multimodal workflows. Across four systems, I explored this question in creative support, video accessibility, physically situated tasks, and knowledge work respectively. Mimosa demonstrated that decomposing an opaque AI pipeline into interpretable, editable stages lets users detect errors, repair them, and creatively augment outputs, improving perceived immersion and creative flexibility without demanding prior expertise. Spica showed that restructuring video accessibility from passive audio description into active, multi-granularity exploration significantly improves both comprehension and engagement for BLV users, and that layered access enables cross-validation of AI-generated content, building trust where independent visual verification is unavailable. Aroma extended multimodal AI assistance into the physical world, revealing that the most effective support occurs when the system validates and extends users' own embodied perceptual inferences rather than substituting for them. Transmogrifier introduced interpretive linking as a mechanism for propagating meaning-preserving edits across heterogeneous representations, demonstrating that generative AI can manage semantic coherence across modalities when changes are kept inspectable and reversible. The deeper contribution is the design implications these systems reflect. Rather than optimizing for model autonomy, the empirical evidence consistently favors optimizing the quality of human-AI coupling: the model contributes scale, speed, and cross-modal transformation; the human contributes embodied expertise, contextual judgment, and perceptual grounding . Building such symbiosis requires advancing not only the AI "brain" but also the multimodal "nervous system" that connects AI capabilities with human cognition.

Rather than optimizing for model autonomy, the empirical evidence consistently favors optimizing the quality of human-AI coupling: the model contributes scale, speed, and cross-modal transformation; the human contributes embodied expertise, contextual judgment, and perceptual grounding . Building such symbiosis requires advancing not only the AI "brain" but also the multimodal "nervous system" that connects AI capabilities with human cognition. This dissertation contributes four instantiations of that coupling layer in different application areas.

section: Error Handling in Human-AI Collaboration: An Empirical Study of Natural Language Database Querying
content: (This chapter is modified from the journal paper: Ning Z, Tian Y, Zhang Z, Zhang T, Li TJ. Insights into natural language database query errors: From attention misalignment to user handling strategies. ACM Transactions on Interactive Intelligent Systems. 2024 Dec 17;14(4):1-32. ) A recurring theme throughout this dissertation is that AI models inevitably produce errors, and effective human-AI systems must provide mechanisms for users to detect, understand, and correct those errors. In Mimosa, an error in object detection propagates through the spatial audio pipeline; in Spica, an incorrect caption misleads a user who lacks independent visual verification; in Aroma, a misidentified cooking state triggers a false alert that erodes trust; and in Transmogrifier, a misinterpreted edit silently alters the communicative intent of a linked artifact. Each of these systems adopts a common design strategy: exposing intermediate AI representations so that users can locate and correct errors rather than receiving only the final output. However, the effectiveness of such strategies depends on how well error-handling mechanisms match the types of errors users actually encounter, and how efficiently users can leverage those mechanisms given their expertise and task context. This appendix presents an empirical investigation of these questions in a controlled setting, using natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) translation as a proxy task. NL2SQL is an ideal testbed because (1) the correctness of model output is objectively verifiable, (2) the task naturally requires human-AI collaboration since current models achieve approximately 85\

section: Error Handling in Human-AI Collaboration: An Empirical Study of Natural Language Database Querying
content: This appendix presents an empirical investigation of these questions in a controlled setting, using natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) translation as a proxy task. NL2SQL is an ideal testbed because (1) the correctness of model output is objectively verifiable, (2) the task naturally requires human-AI collaboration since current models achieve approximately 85\ This work makes four contributions: (i) a detailed taxonomy of NL2SQL model error types derived through qualitative coding of failure cases across multiple model architectures, (ii) empirical evidence linking SQL generation errors to attention misalignment between model focus and human comprehension, (iii) a controlled user study (N=26) evaluating the effectiveness of three representative approaches for interactive error discovery and repair, and (iv) design implications for future interactive systems that support human reasoning about AI-generated outputs, which directly informed the design of the systems presented in this dissertation.

Supporting natural language queries for relational databases is a long-standing problem in both the database and NLP communities. Given a relational database D and a natural language query q_nl, an NL2SQL model finds an equivalent SQL statement q_sql to answer q_nl. Early methods depended on intermediate logical representations or handcrafted mapping rules , both of which required significant manual effort and covered only a limited set of semantically tractable queries . Recent deep-learning-based methods learn NL2SQL translation from large-scale datasets in a data-driven manner . Most adopt encoder-decoder architectures, where the encoder maps q_nl into hidden representations and the decoder generates the corresponding SQL . Transformer-based encoders and pre-training techniques have become the dominant approach.

Prior work has explored multiple paradigms for supporting error detection and repair in NL2SQL through human-AI collaboration. For error detection, a common approach is to explain the query in natural language: DIY and STEPS show step-by-step explanations by decomposing queries into subqueries; NaLIR explains entity mappings between the input query and the database schema. Visualization-based approaches such as QueryVis , QUEST , and SQLVis use diagrams to represent query structures. For error repair, direct manipulation approaches allow users to modify entity mappings through drop-down menus or adjust quantitative values through sliders . Conversational approaches such as MISP enable users to provide feedback through dialog to iteratively improve SQL predictions. Despite individual evaluations showing promise, it remains unclear how effective these mechanisms are when applied to errors made by state-of-the-art models on complex, cross-domain datasets, or how user expertise modulates their effectiveness. This work bridges that gap through a controlled comparative study.

Handling AI errors in collaborative settings faces several key challenges . First, many models lack transparency, making it difficult for users to understand what led to incorrect predictions. Although methods exist to partially expose model reasoning, including heatmaps , search traces , and natural language explanations , they typically reveal only snapshots of the model's state rather than a holistic view. Second, representational mismatch makes it difficult for users to build accurate mental models of complex AI systems. Third, effective error handling often requires coherent multi-turn interactions , which remain challenging for AI systems to maintain . These challenges echo findings across other human-AI collaboration domains, including programming , data annotation , and interactive task learning , confirming that users need better support for comprehending AI model states and for forming appropriate mental models. This observation directly motivated the transparency-oriented design principles adopted throughout this dissertation.

We adopted the Spider dataset, a large-scale benchmark for complex, cross-domain NL2SQL parsing. The original dataset contains approximately 10,000 queries across multiple databases. We focused on the first three difficulty levels (Easy, Medium, Hard), excluding "Extra Hard" queries because state-of-the-art models achieve significantly lower accuracy on them (e. g. , SmBop achieves only approximately 50\ Since Spider's held-out test set is not publicly available, we re-split the public training and development sets into 5,483 training and 2,288 test samples, maintaining similar difficulty-level ratios and ensuring no database overlap between splits. Table [ref] shows the distribution. We retrained three representative NL2SQL models from the Spider leaderboard, selected for their diverse architectures and publicly available code. SmBop uses a bottom-up decoding structure that incrementally combines smaller SQL components. BRIDGE employs a BERT-based encoder with a sequential pointer-generator for end-to-end semantic parsing. GAZP combines a semantic parser with an utterance generator to adapt to new databases via synthesized data. Table [ref] shows each model's accuracy and the number of erroneous queries collected.

We followed established open, axial, and iterative coding procedures to develop a taxonomy of NL2SQL errors. Step 1: Open coding. Four authors with in-depth SQL knowledge performed open coding on a random sample of 40 erroneous SQL queries. They coded each query to identify: (1) the errors in comparison to the ground truth, (2) the SQL component each error resides in, and (3) whether all errors in the query were covered. After independent coding, annotators discussed codes collaboratively to reach consensus on a preliminary codebook. Step 2: Iterative refinement. Over three additional iterations, each with a new batch of 40 queries, annotators applied the current codebook, computed inter-rater reliability (Fleiss' Kappa and Krippendorff's Alpha), and collaboratively refined the codes. By the final iteration, Fleiss' Kappa reached 0. 69 and Krippendorff's Alpha reached 0. 67, indicating substantial agreement. Step 3: Coding the remaining dataset. Two annotators then coded the remaining dataset using the finalized codebook, achieving Fleiss' Kappa of 0. 76 and Krippendorff's Alpha of 0. 78 . The annotation interface (Figure [ref]) displayed the natural language query, the ground truth SQL, and the model-generated SQL, alongside the relevant database tables and query results.

Table [ref] shows the finalized taxonomy. Errors are categorized along two dimensions: (1) the syntactic dimension indicates which SQL keyword the error occurs in (e. g. , WHERE, JOIN), and (2) the semantic dimension indicates which aspect of the natural language the model misunderstands (e. g. , table, column, value). Each error type is identified by an uppercase letter (syntactic category) and a lowercase letter (semantic category). An erroneous query may exhibit multiple error types.
Based on the taxonomy, we investigated three questions about the characteristics of NL2SQL errors.

section: The distribution of erroneous queries among models
content: Figure [ref] shows the overlap of erroneous queries among the three models. Notably, 81. 3\ To assess whether models make similar types of errors on overlapping queries, we computed the Jaccard distance of error types for each shared erroneous query. For syntactic errors, 21.

The distributions of syntactic error types are similar across models. WHERE, JOIN, and SELECT errors account for the highest percentage of erroneous queries. However, a higher frequency of errors does not equate to a higher error rate for a specific keyword. For example, UNION errors account for fewer than 3\ Compared to syntactic errors, the distribution of semantic errors is more varied between models: d: Value error is the most frequent for SmBop (35.

section: Distance between erroneous and ground truth queries
content: We measured the Levenshtein distance (minimum word-level edits) between erroneous queries and their ground truths (Figure [ref]). All three distributions have a long tail, but a large portion of erroneous queries can be fixed with a small number of edits. For instance, 19.

After characterizing the error types and distribution, we investigated a potential cause of NL2SQL errors. Prior research has shown that misalignment between model attention and human attention correlates with poor performance in code summarization , machine translation , and visual feature representation . We hypothesized that such misalignment is also a source of error in NL2SQL: models return incorrect columns or tables in part because they do not focus on the words that humans consider important.

We selected SmBop for the attention analysis because it achieves the second-highest performance among our reproduced models, and unlike GPT-4, it is open-source and allows tractable attention computation. Due to SmBop's complex architecture (bottom-up decoding with transformer-based contextualization and rule-based filtering), self-attention or gradient-based methods cannot accurately represent the attention of the entire model. We therefore adopted a perturbation-based method that treats SmBop as a black box, replacing tokens with contextually similar alternatives predicted by a masked language model (BERT ) and measuring the resulting changes in model output.

Two annotators proficient in SQL conducted iterative refinements of attention annotation on a balanced sample of 200 tasks from the Spider dataset (140 correctly solved, 60 incorrectly solved by SmBop). In each iteration, annotators independently reviewed the NL question and ground truth SQL, marking words that contribute to understanding the query as important (attention weight = 1) and others as unimportant (attention weight = 0). After three refinement iterations with inter-rater reliability checks , Fleiss' Kappa reached 0. 67 and Krippendorff's Alpha reached 0. 58.

We measured alignment using the keyword coverage rate: for the top K model-focused words (where K equals the number of human-labeled keywords), we computed the proportion of human-labeled words covered: rate = |word_h|  Since model attention is distributed over subword tokens while human labels are applied to whole words, we mapped model token-level attention back to word-level attention by summing attention scores of all tokens overlapping with each word.

section: Finding 1: Model attention partially aligns with human attention.
content: When considering the top 12 words (the average query length), 77.

section: Finding 2: Attention alignment is significantly higher for correctly solved tasks.
content: Figure [ref] compares the keyword coverage rate distributions between correctly and incorrectly solved tasks at K=6. Both distributions approximate a Gaussian, but the mean for erroneous queries is significantly lower (p = 1. 5 10^-4). This pattern holds across all tested K values (all p < 0. 05), providing strong evidence that NL2SQL errors correlate with attention misalignment between humans and models.

section: Finding 3: Attention misalignment is not specific to any error type.
content: We computed the average keyword coverage rate for each error type across the 40 incorrectly solved tasks. No significant differences in attention alignment were found across error types, suggesting that misalignment is a general phenomenon affecting all categories of NL2SQL errors rather than being localized to specific syntactic or semantic failures.

section: User Study: Interactive Error Discovery and Repair
content: To understand how effectively users can leverage existing error-handling mechanisms to address NL2SQL errors, we conducted a within-subjects controlled study with 26 participants. The study investigated three research questions: - RQ1. : How effective and efficient are different error-handling mechanisms and interaction strategies in NL2SQL? - RQ2. : What are user preferences and perceptions of different mechanisms? - RQ3. : What gaps exist between the capabilities of existing approaches and user needs?

Participants included SQL users at three expertise levels: experts (using SQL weekly or more), intermediates (using SQL occasionally), and novices (limited SQL experience). We selected three representative error-handling approaches: - B1 (Baseline): A text editor for directly editing the SQL query, providing the NL question, generated SQL, and database schema. - C1 (Explanation and example-based): Based on DIY , this approach supports error detection through step-by-step NL explanations of subqueries and error repair through drop-down menus for entity mapping corrections. - C2 (Visualization-based): Based on SQLVis , this approach provides a graph-based visualization of the SQL query structure to help users understand syntactic relationships. - C3 (Conversational dialog): Based on MISP , this approach enables error detection and repair through human-AI conversation, where the model asks clarification questions and updates predictions based on user feedback. Each participant completed four blocks of tasks, one per condition (B1, C1, C2, C3), with condition order counterbalanced using a Latin square design. Each block contained three erroneous SQL queries from the error taxonomy, balanced across difficulty levels.

section: F1: Error-handling mechanisms do not significantly improve accuracy.
content: A one-way ANOVA (=0. 05) showed no significant differences in accuracy across conditions (p=0. 82). The average accuracy ranged from 0. 53 (C3) to 0. 60 (C2), with the baseline at 0. 55. Similarly, no significant accuracy differences were found when analyzing by specific error types or difficulty levels (all p > 0. 05). These findings suggest that existing interactive mechanisms are not highly effective for handling errors that state-of-the-art NL2SQL models make on complex, cross-domain datasets.

section: F2: Error-handling mechanisms do not significantly impact completion time.
content: The overall time of completion (ToC) did not differ significantly across conditions (p=0. 52). However, for easy queries, there was a significant effect (p=0. 04): direct editing (B1) was fastest, while the conversational dialog approach (C3) was slowest, suggesting that for simpler errors, the overhead of interactive mechanisms outweighs their benefit.

section: F3: Users perform better on error types with fewer variants.
content: Users achieved higher accuracy on "local" error types such as value errors and condition errors (e. g. , wrong comparator), which typically require fewer edits and have less interdependency with other parts of the query. In contrast, "semantically complicated" errors involving joins across multiple tables and column selections from different schemas posed greater challenges. This finding is significant for the design of the main dissertation systems: it suggests that effective error-handling mechanisms should decompose complex errors into simpler, more localized sub-problems, a principle adopted in Mimosa's pipeline decomposition and Spica's layered information disclosure.

section: F4: Explanation-based methods are most useful for non-expert users.
content: Participants rated C1 (explanation and example-based) as the most useful overall.

section: F5: Explanation-based methods are more effective for error discovery than repair.
content: Participants found C1 helpful for identifying erroneous parts of the SQL query, particularly through recursive NL explanations that decomposed complex queries into understandable subqueries. However, the repair capabilities were limited: users could only replace incorrect entities through drop-down menus, but many errors required adding, deleting, or reorganizing clauses. As one participant noted: "I did not trust my own edits nor the suggested changes from the approach" (P20). The visualization-based approach (C2) similarly helped with understanding query structure but provided no direct repair functionality.

section: Improving NL2SQL model evaluation through error taxonomies
content: Current NL2SQL benchmarks report only aggregate accuracy, obscuring model-specific weaknesses. The error taxonomy contributed by this work enables fine-grained comparison: despite different architectures and overall performance, all models exhibit high error rates on the same syntactic categories. Simultaneously, models with lower overall accuracy may handle specific error types better than higher-performing models. This insight supports a broader principle adopted throughout the dissertation: that understanding how AI systems fail, not just how often, is essential for designing effective human-AI collaboration.

section: Designing error-handling mechanisms grounded in attention alignment
content: The attention analysis demonstrates that NL2SQL errors correlate with misalignment between model and human focus. This finding motivates designing interactive error-handling mechanisms that make attention visible and manipulable. In fact, existing interactive mechanisms can be reframed as implicit attention alignment: MISP asks clarification questions when uncertain about a token, effectively seeking to realign its focus with the user's intent. The attention analysis suggests that making this alignment explicit through visualizations of model focus, could enable more targeted error correction. This principle directly informs the design of Mimosa (exposing intermediate pipeline stages) and Transmogrifier (providing visual traceability of how changes propagate).

A central finding of the user study is that existing mechanisms support error discovery more effectively than error repair. This asymmetry motivates mixed-initiative approaches where both the human and the system contribute to resolving errors, a paradigm that the main dissertation systems adopt. In Aroma, the system proactively monitors cooking state and raises alerts, while the user provides embodied perceptual knowledge that the system cannot access. In Spica, users control the depth and timing of information disclosure, while the system generates descriptions and spatial cues. The NL2SQL study shows that such bidirectional collaboration is necessary because neither fully automated correction (as in C3's chatbot) nor fully manual repair (as in B1's text editor) is sufficient.

section: Adapting strategies to user expertise and error context
content: The study reveals that the most effective strategy depends on user expertise, query complexity, and error type. Expert users benefit less from explanation-based approaches but value execution previews and validation tools. Non-expert users rely heavily on explanations that lower the barrier to understanding AI outputs. This finding supports the design of adaptive interfaces, as implemented in Spica (frame-level vs. object-level exploration) and Aroma (on-demand vs. proactive assistance), which adjust information density and initiative level based on user needs.

The study has several limitations. First, the unbalanced distribution of error types in the Spider dataset means some rare error types could not be fully examined. Second, while we reproduced three representative models, the rapidly evolving NL2SQL landscape means newer models (e. g. , those based on large language models such as GPT-4) may exhibit different error patterns. Third, the attention analysis was conducted on only one model (SmBop) due to computational constraints. Fourth, the study setting, while using real model errors on realistic databases, lacked the task context of actual NL2SQL usage scenarios. Future work should address these limitations through larger-scale studies, analysis of LLM-based NL2SQL models, and field deployments that capture naturalistic error-handling behaviors.

This appendix presented an empirical investigation of error handling in NL2SQL as a proxy for the broader challenge of human-AI collaboration that underpins this dissertation. The study demonstrated three key results. First, despite differing architectures, NL2SQL models produce errors from a common set of challenging queries, and the 48-type error taxonomy reveals that semantic challenges are more varied than syntactic ones. Second, SQL generation errors strongly correlate with attention misalignment between models and humans, suggesting that aligning model focus with human comprehension is a promising direction for improving both model accuracy and interactive error correction. Third, existing error-handling mechanisms support error discovery more effectively than repair, and their utility depends on user expertise and error complexity. These findings directly inform the design principles applied throughout the main dissertation: (1) exposing intermediate AI representations so users can inspect and correct errors at the level where they occur, (2) supporting multiple granularities of interaction to accommodate diverse expertise levels, and (3) designing mixed-initiative systems where both human judgment and AI capability contribute to resolving errors. Together, the NL2SQL study and the four systems presented in the main chapters demonstrate that effective human-AI collaboration requires neither fully automated solutions nor fully manual intervention, but rather carefully designed interactive mechanisms that leverage the complementary strengths of humans and AI.

Effectively incorporating diverse content into our everyday workflows is cognitively demanding, especially when modalities are incomplete, inaccessible, or misaligned with how humans naturally process information .  In this dissertation, I present four system work investigating how AI-enabled interactive systems can augment human cognition in such multimodal settings. Specifically, these systems explore opportunities for AI to enhance user creativity, efficiency, and trust by enabling cross-modal content transformation, compensating for missing sensory modalities, and reducing the coordination burden of working across multiple representations. First, I present Mimosa, a prototype that investigates how AI models can blend into spatial audio effect creation for videos, which requires synergy between visual and auditory cognitive process. Mimosa decomposes this workflow into a human-AI collaborative pipeline that consists of interpretable, editable stages, improving users efficiency by allowing them to build on top of the AI generated "baseline" results. Next, I study scenarios where one modality in user perception is absent or impaired, and choose video consumption for blind or low-vision (BLV) users as the lens for this problem space. I present Spica, a system that transforms passive video consumption experience through audio descriptions (ADs) into active, self-directed exploration for BLV users through layered audio descriptions and spatial sound cues at both frame and object levels.

Next, I study scenarios where one modality in user perception is absent or impaired, and choose video consumption for blind or low-vision (BLV) users as the lens for this problem space. I present Spica, a system that transforms passive video consumption experience through audio descriptions (ADs) into active, self-directed exploration for BLV users through layered audio descriptions and spatial sound cues at both frame and object levels. Going beyond videos on 2D screens, in the follow-up work, I build Aroma, a system that extends multimodal perception into physical interaction in the real world. Aroma brings AI assistance into cooking, one of the critical activities of daily living for BLV people, by pairing BLV users' embodied perceptions with an agent that provides on-demand guidance and proactive state monitoring during cooking. Finally, to understand the broader challenge of working with cross-modal content such as images, text in knowledge work and explore the feasibility of blending multimodal interaction such as sketch, type into the workflow, I design and build Transmogrifier, a design probe that supports dynamic, semantically grounded transformations across text, charts, and images, enabling users to maintain coherence across linked representations as content evolves. Evaluations across user studies demonstrate that these systems effectively reduce cognitive load, preserve user agency, and support reliable human-AI collaboration in multimodal workflows. The design of these systems is grounded in foundational theories of human-computer interaction, AI, and cognitive science, and shares overarching design goals around error handling in human-AI systems, user agency and trust, and adaptive considerations for accommodating the cognitive capabilities and needs of diverse user groups. Together, the systems and their findings offer design insights for future multimodal human-AI systems.

The design of these systems is grounded in foundational theories of human-computer interaction, AI, and cognitive science, and shares overarching design goals around error handling in human-AI systems, user agency and trust, and adaptive considerations for accommodating the cognitive capabilities and needs of diverse user groups. Together, the systems and their findings offer design insights for future multimodal human-AI systems. In closing the dissertation, I reflect on how to progress toward a new "man-computer symbiosis" in the generative AI era, one grounded in JCR Licklider's 1960 vision, and advanced through data-driven intelligence improvement alongside human-centered evaluation and interface design. Data querying is an indispensable step in data analysis, sensemaking, and decision-making processes. However, traditional data query interfaces require users to specify their queries in a formal language such as SQL, leading to significant learning barriers for non-expert users who have little programming experience . This problem becomes increasingly important in the Big Data era, given the rising needs for end users in many key domains including business, healthcare, public policy, scientific research, etc. To address this problem, natural language (NL) data query interfaces allow users to express data queries in natural language. For example, a semantic parser can map the user's natural language query into a formal data query language such as SQL (NL2SQL). Such NL interfaces have shown the potential to lower the bar for data querying and support flexible data exploration for end users . However, achieving robust NL2SQL parsing in realistic scenarios is difficult because of the ambiguity in natural language and the complex structures (e. g. , nested queries, joined queries) in the target queries. For example, in Spider , a large-scale complex and cross-domain dataset for NL2SQL parsing, the accuracy of state-of-the-art models remained low in the 20\ This work focuses on the flip side of the 85\ We first performed a comprehensive analysis of SQL errors made by representative state-of-the-art NL2SQL models and developed an axial taxonomy of those errors. We reproduced four representative high-performing models with various structures from the Spider leaderboard (---DIN-SQL + GPT-4 , SmBop + GraPPa (SmBop) , BRIDGE v2 + BERT (BRIDGE) , and GAZP + BERT (GAZP) . For each model, we collected all model-generated queries for the Spider dataset whose execution results varied from the ground truth results. Four authors conducted multiple rounds of qualitative coding and refinement on these errors to derive a taxonomy of the errors.

For each model, we collected all model-generated queries for the Spider dataset whose execution results varied from the ground truth results. Four authors conducted multiple rounds of qualitative coding and refinement on these errors to derive a taxonomy of the errors. The error analysis reveals that, despite having different model architectures and performance, NL2SQL errors originate from a common set of queries and demonstrate a similar distribution across various error types.  Given the error distribution, we further investigate the potential reasons behind the error. Inspired by recent work , which showed that attention alignment can improve the performance in code summarization, machine translation, and visual feature representation, we hypothesize that SQL errors generated by NL2SQL models derive from the misalignment between the model's attention and human's attention toward the natural language query. To validate this hypothesis, two authors (SQL experts) manually annotated important words (human attention) in the NL queries when they try to understand the query. The model-focused words (model attention) were obtained by calculating the weight of each word that contributed to the model's prediction using a perturbation-based method . The attention alignment was measured by computing the overlap between the human-focused words and the model-focused words. The results showed a significant difference in attention alignment between erroneous queries and correctly predicted queries, implying that NL2SQL errors are highly correlated with attention misalignment. The findings suggest the promise of future work in aligning model attention with human attention to improve model performance. To support NL2SQL models in real-world deployment, it is also important to provide effective error-handling mechanisms for users, which enable human users to discover and repair errors.

The findings suggest the promise of future work in aligning model attention with human attention to improve model performance. To support NL2SQL models in real-world deployment, it is also important to provide effective error-handling mechanisms for users, which enable human users to discover and repair errors. In both human-computer interaction (HCI) and natural language processing (NLP) communities, we have seen efforts using different approaches. In general, there are three representative paradigms. First, following the task decomposition paradigm, strategies such as DIY decompose a generated SQL statement into a sequence of incremental subqueries and provide step-by-step explanations to help users identify errors. Second, based on query visualization, approaches such as QueryVis , QUEST , and SQLVis seek to improve user understanding of the generated SQL statements by visualizing the structures and relations among entities and tables in a query. Third, using conversational agents, works such as implement chatbots to communicate the model's current state with users and update the results with user feedback through dialogs. These interactive approaches help the model and humans to synchronize their attention. Consequently, humans gain confidence in the decisions generated by the model, while the models can make better decisions under human guidance. Although these approaches were shown to be useful in different contexts in individual evaluations, it is unclear how effective each approach is for users with various SQL expertise. Furthermore, as most of these methods were evaluated with only simple NL2SQL errors, it is unclear how well they will perform on errors made by state-of-the-art NL2SQL models on complex datasets such as Spider. Therefore, we selected three representative models and conducted a controlled user study (N=26) to investigate the effectiveness and efficiency of representative error discovery and repair approaches.

Furthermore, as most of these methods were evaluated with only simple NL2SQL errors, it is unclear how well they will perform on errors made by state-of-the-art NL2SQL models on complex datasets such as Spider. Therefore, we selected three representative models and conducted a controlled user study (N=26) to investigate the effectiveness and efficiency of representative error discovery and repair approaches. Specifically, we selected (i) an explanation- and example-based approach that supports fixing the SQL query through entity mapping between the natural language (NL) question and the generated query and discovering the error through a step-by-step NL explanation approach (DIY ), (ii) an explanation-based SQL visualization approach (SQLVis ), and (iii) a conversational dialog approach . The study reveals that these error-handling mechanisms have limited impacts on increasing the efficiency and accuracy of error discovery and repair for errors made by state-of-the-art NL2SQL models. Finally, we discussed the implications for future error-handling mechanisms in NL query interfaces. To conclude, this paper presents the following four contributions: [nosep] - We developed a taxonomy of error types for three representative state-of-the-art NL2SQL models through iterative and axial coding procedures. - We conducted a comprehensive analysis that compared model attention to human attention. The result shows that NL2SQL errors are highly correlated with attention misalignment between humans and models. - We conducted a controlled user study that investigated the effectiveness and efficiency of three representative NL2SQL error discovery and repair methods. - We discussed the implications for designing future error-handling mechanisms in natural language query interfaces.

Supporting natural language queries for relational databases is a long-standing problem in both the database (DB) and NLP communities. Given a relational database D and a natural language query q_nl to D, an NL2SQL model aims to find an equivalent SQL statement q_sql to answer q_nl. The early methods of mapping q_nl to q_sql depend mainly on the development of intermediate logical representation or mapping rules . In the former case, q_nl is first parsed into logical queries independent of the underlying database schema, which are then converted into queries that can be executed on the target database . On the contrary, rule-based methods generally assume that there is a one-to-one correspondence between the words in q_nl and a subset of database keywords/entities . Therefore, the NL2SQL mapping can be achieved by directly applying the syntactic parsing and semantic entity mapping rules to q_nl. Although both strategies have achieved significant improvement over time, they have two intrinsic limitations. First, they require significant effort to create hand-crafted mapping rules for translation . Second, the coverage of these methods is limited to a definite set of semantically tractable natural language queries .   The recent development of deep learning (DL) based methods aims to achieve flexible NL2SQL translation through a data-driven approach . From large-scale datasets, DL-based models learn to interpret NL queries conditioned on a relational DB via SQL logic . Most NL2SQL models use the encoder-decoder architecture , where the encoder models the input q_nl into a sequence of hidden representations along time steps. The decoder then maps the hidden representations into the corresponding SQL statement. Recently, Transformer-based architecture and pre-training techniques have become popular as the backbone of NL2SQL encoders.

The decoder then maps the hidden representations into the corresponding SQL statement. Recently, Transformer-based architecture and pre-training techniques have become popular as the backbone of NL2SQL encoders. At the same time, many decoders have been used to optimize SQL generation, such as autoregressive bottom-up decoding and the LSTM-based pointer-generator . However, those DL-based models are usually "black-boxes" due to the lack of explainability . The lack of transparency makes it difficult for users to figure out how to fix the observed errors when using DL-based NL2SQL models.   The evaluation of these DL-based models is based mainly on objective benchmarks such as Spider and WikiSQL . For example, Spider requires models to generalize well not only to unseen NL queries but also to new database schemas, in order to encourage NL interfaces to adapt to cross-domain databases. The performance of a model is evaluated using multiple measures, including component matching, exact matching, and execution accuracy. However, these benchmarks only involve quantitative analysis of NL2SQL models, giving little clue about what types of errors a model tends to fall into.  An aim of our work is to develop a taxonomy of error types of errors made by state-of-the-art NL2SQL models and report the corresponding descriptive statistics to complement the quantitative benchmark with the qualitative analysis of those NL2SQL models.

section: Detecting and repairing errors for NL2SQL
content: Natural language interfaces for NL2SQL face challenges in language ambiguity, underspecification, and model misunderstanding . Previous work has explored ways to support error detection and repair for NL2SQL systems through human-AI collaboration. NL2SQL error detection methods can be mainly divided into categories of natural language-explanation-based, visualization-based, and conversation-based approaches. NL2SQL error repair methods consist mainly of direct manipulation and conversational error fixing approaches.  For error detection, a popular method is to explain the query and its answer in natural language . For example, DIY and STEPS show step-by-step NL explanations and query results by applying templates to subqueries, helping users understand SQL output in an incremental way; similarly, SOVITE allows users to fix agent breakdowns in understanding user instructions by revealing the agent's current state of understanding of the user's intent and supporting direct manipulation for the user to repair the detected errors; NaLIR explains the mapping relations between entities in the input query and those in the database schema; Ioannidis et al. introduced a technique to describe structured database content and SQL translation textually to support user sensemaking of the model output. Visualizations have also been widely used to explain a SQL query and its execution . For example, QueryVis produces diagrams of SQL queries to capture their logical structures; QUEST visualizes the connection between the matching entities from the input query and their correspondences in the database schema; SQLVis introduced visual query representations to help SQL users understand the complex structure of SQL queries and verify their correctness.  Most of the previous work employed direct manipulation to repair and disambiguate queries.

section: Detecting and repairing errors for NL2SQL
content: Most of the previous work employed direct manipulation to repair and disambiguate queries. NaLIR , DIY and DataTone allow users to modify the entity mappings through drop-downs; Eviza and Orko enable users to modify quantitative values in queries through range sliders. In addition to direct manipulation, several other prior interaction mechanisms enable users to give feedback to NL2SQL models through dialogs in natural language. For example, MISP maintains the state of the current parsing process and asks for human feedback to improve SQL translation through human-AI conversations. Elgohary et al. investigated how to enable users to correct NL2SQL parsing results with natural language feedback in conversation.   With the many error-handling mechanisms that have been proposed, there is a gap in evaluating how effective and efficient these mechanisms are to address different types of NL2SQL errors and what specific limitations they have. These types of information are critical to inform the effective choice and design of NL2SQL error handling mechanisms in different use scenarios and to inspire the use of ensemble mechanisms to handle different usage contexts of NL2SQL. Our work bridges this gap by investigating these questions through controlled user studies, whose findings could guide the future design of NL2SQL error handling systems.

Handling errors made by AI models in human-AI collaboration faces many key challenges. First, many state-of-the-art AI models lack transparency in their decision-making process, making it difficult for users to understand exactly what leads to incorrect predictions . Although there are some attempts to explain the state of the AI model using methods such as heatmap , search traces , and natural language explanations , they only allow users to peek at the AI model's reasoning at certain stages instead of exposing the holistic states of the model. Second, it is difficult for users to develop a correct mental model for complex AI models due to the representational mismatch in which " humans can create a mental model in terms of features that are not identical to those used by AI models" . Lastly, error handling usually requires multiple turns of interactions . However, maintaining coherent multi-turn interactions between AI and humans is challenging . It requires AI to closely maintain and update the context history, evolve its contextual understanding, and behave appropriately based on user's timely responses .  Our work contributes to the knowledge of how users handle errors in their collaborations with NL2SQL models by studying how users utilize existing error-handling mechanisms to inspect and fix errors made by NL2SQL models and how they perceive the usefulness of these mechanisms. Our findings of user challenges also echo the identified challenges in human-AI collaborations in other domains (e. g. , programming , data annotation , QA generation , interactive task learning ), showing that users need help comprehending the state of AI models and developing a proper mental model in AI-based interactive data-centric tools to understand and assess their recommendations.

section: An Analysis and Taxonomy of NL2SQL Errors
content: In this section, we describe the development of the taxonomy of NL2SQL errors of four representative NL2SQL models and the corresponding error analysis. The structure of this section is as follows. Section [ref] introduces the models we selected for analyzing the erroneous SQL queries. We also discussed the discrepancy in the workflow of different models. Section [ref] summarizes the methodology used for building the dataset; Section [ref] explains the axial and iterative coding procedure we used to derive the error taxonomy; Section [ref] describes the developed taxonomy of NL2SQL errors; Section [ref] presents an analysis of erroneous queries in the dataset based on the taxonomy.

We selected four representative NL2SQL models from the official Spider leader board, the information of which is shown in Table [ref].  While most models generate the SQL query in a top-down decoding procedure, SmBop (M1) improves the speed and accuracy by adopting a bottom-up structure. Specifically, it gradually combines smaller components to form a syntactically larger SQL component. BRIDGE (M2) is a sequential architecture that models the dependencies between natural language queries and relational databases. It combines a BERT-based encoder with a sequential pointer-generator for end-to-end cross-DB NL2SQL semantic parsing. In comparison, GAZP (M3) combines a semantic parser with an utterance generator. When given a new database, it synthesizes data and selects instances that are consistent with the schema to adapt an existing semantic parser to this new database. DIN-SQL+GPT-4 (M4) has the best accuracy among all four models. It improves the performance of the large language model (GPT-4 ) on text-to-SQL tasks by employing task decomposition, adaptive prompting, linking schema to prompt, and self-correction. All these models employ different NL2SQL task-solving strategies at different stages, including decoding (M1), encoding (M2), finetuning (M3), and task preprocessing (M4).

We adopted the Spider dataset to train and evaluate the models and to collect a set of erroneous SQL queries for the taxonomy. Spider is the most popular benchmark to evaluate NL2SQL models with complex and cross-domain semantic parsing problems. It consists of around 10,000 queries in natural language on multiple databases across different domains (e. g. , "soccer", "college"). In the original Spider dataset, the difficulty of the queries is divided into four levels: "Easy", "Medium", "Hard", and "Extra Hard", depending on the complexity of their structures and the SQL keywords involved. We demonstrate an example NL-SQL pair for each difficulty level in Table [ref]. In this work, we focus only on the first three difficulty levels as state-of-the-art models have significantly lower accuracy on the "extra hard" queries --- the best model we reproduced, DIN-SQL+GPT-4, only achieved less than 50\

Channel WHERE T2. directed_by = \\'Michael Chang' INTERSECT SELECT T1. series_name , T1. country \ TV_Channel AS T1 JOIN cartoon AS T2 ON T1. id = T2.
